proposals.title: Beyond the Gold Standard: Quantifying Expert Disagreement and Its Impact on Multilingual Speech Model Alignment
proposals.motivation: The rapid development of multilingual foundation models for speech and language relies heavily on expert-annotated datasets for fine-tuning and alignment, assuming these labels represent a singular, optimal truth. This assumption is increasingly problematic in high-stakes, subjective domains like sentiment, emotion, or hate speech detection, where expert disagreement is inherent and reflects legitimate ambiguity. Grounded in pre-2026 concerns about fairness and accountability in models for policing and content moderation, this work challenges the uncritical use of expert consensus as a training target.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the distribution of expert annotations, rather than collapsing them to a single gold label, will produce more robust, calibrated, and ethically aligned multilingual speech models, particularly in subjective classification tasks.
proposals.method: We will curate or adapt existing multilingual speech datasets (e.g., emotion recognition) where multiple expert annotations per utterance are available. We will train and compare two model pipelines: a baseline trained on majority-vote expert labels, and a proposed model trained using a distributional loss (e.g., KL divergence) to match the full distribution of expert labels. The alignment stage will utilize a prospect-theoretic optimization framework (inspired by KTO) that incorporates label uncertainty directly into the reward model, moving beyond simple preference pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, quantitative framework for integrating expert disagreement into model training pipelines, moving the field beyond the 'gold standard' paradigm. It will yield concrete evidence on whether embracing annotation ambiguity improves model robustness and fairness in subjective, multilingual speech tasks relevant to real-world applications.
proposals.novelty: While dataset studies note inter-annotator disagreement, few pre-2026 works systematically use the full distribution of expert labels to train and align models, especially in the speech domain. The novelty lies in combining distributional training with prospect-theoretic alignment for multilingual speech, directly addressing the 'fair use' of ambiguous human judgments in foundation models.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Data-Centric Paradigm: A Multilingual Audit of Task Complexity and Linguistic Phenomena in Crowd-Sourced NLP
proposals.motivation: The NLP field increasingly relies on crowd-sourced data for training and evaluation, assuming it provides uniformly reliable signals across diverse tasks. This assumption underpins major benchmarks and the development of foundation models, yet systematic evidence for its validity is lacking. If false, it could introduce hidden biases in model performance assessments, particularly for complex linguistic phenomena or low-resource languages, impacting fairness and accountability in applications.
proposals.hypothesis: The suitability and reliability of crowd-sourced annotations are not uniform; they degrade predictably with increasing task complexity (e.g., discourse-level reasoning vs. lexical similarity) and for specific, subtle linguistic phenomena (e.g., implicature, code-switching) compared to simpler tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled, multilingual benchmark spanning a typology of tasks (classification, generation, structured prediction) and linguistic phenomena (syntax, semantics, pragmatics, sociolinguistics). Using a platform like Amazon Mechanical Turk, we will collect annotations for each task-phenomenon pair across multiple languages (e.g., English, Spanish, Mandarin, Swahili). We will analyze inter-annotator agreement and compare crowd-sourced labels to expert 'gold' labels, using statistical models (e.g., mixed-effects regression) to quantify the impact of task complexity, phenomenon type, and language resource level on data quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for auditing the 'crowd-suitability' of NLP tasks, yielding concrete guidelines for when expert annotation is necessary. It will contribute a diagnostic benchmark for data quality and inform more robust, equitable dataset creation practices for multilingual and sociolinguistically complex applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines annotator bias, no study has systematically decoupled and measured the independent effects of broad linguistic phenomena and formal task complexity on crowd-sourcing viability, especially within a multilingual context that connects to fairness and resource disparity concerns.

proposals.title: Generalizing Bias Correction for Human Annotation: A Resource-Efficient Framework for Multilingual and Multimodal NLP Tasks
proposals.motivation: Systematic biases in human annotation, such as annotator demographic skew or task-specific framing effects, propagate into training data and degrade model fairness and performance, especially for multilingual and low-resource settings. Pre-2026 bias correction techniques are often task-specific, limiting their broad applicability and scalability across the diverse annotation paradigms used in modern NLP and speech processing. This creates a critical barrier to developing equitable and robust language technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified, prospect-theoretic bias correction framework, initially validated on limited tasks, can be effectively generalized to a wider range of NLP and speech annotation tasks—including sentiment analysis, hate speech detection, and speech emotion recognition—by modeling annotator decision-making as a resource-rational process under uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will extend the bias correction technique from recent work (e.g., KTO's prospect-theoretic modeling of annotator preferences) by developing a task-agnostic, multilingual pipeline. The method involves: 1) Curating a suite of existing annotated datasets across diverse tasks (text classification, sequence labeling, speech tagging) with known demographic metadata. 2) Training a lightweight, interpretable model (e.g., a calibrated linear model or small transformer) to predict annotation outcomes from both input features and annotator metadata. 3) Applying a post-hoc correction layer, inspired by prospect theory, to adjust training labels, decoupling systematic annotator bias from true signal. We will evaluate on held-out annotator groups and against fairness metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, resource-efficient toolkit for debiasing training data across multiple modalities and languages, directly supporting the development of fairer speech processing pipelines and foundation models. It will yield a publicly released multilingual benchmark of bias-corrected datasets and establish empirical guidelines for when task-agnostic correction succeeds or fails.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 debiasing work targets model outputs or specific biases (e.g., gender), this proposal uniquely focuses on generalizing a human-annotator-centric correction method across tasks, framing bias as a consistent cognitive phenomenon in annotation performance rather than a dataset-specific artifact.

proposals.title: Crowd-Sourced Competence: A Systematic Evaluation of Non-Expert vs. Expert Labels for Training Multilingual Speech and Language Models
proposals.motivation: The reliance on expert annotations for training high-performance NLP and speech models creates significant bottlenecks in cost, scalability, and domain accessibility, especially for multilingual and low-resource tasks. While crowdsourcing is common for affect recognition, its efficacy for more complex linguistic tasks—like semantic role labeling, discourse parsing, or code-switching detection—remains underexplored. This research directly addresses the practical challenge of building robust, multilingual models in resource-constrained scenarios, a core theme in pre-2026 NLP.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a range of structured language understanding tasks, models trained on high-agreement non-expert labels will achieve performance statistically indistinguishable from models trained on expert labels, provided the annotation interface and task design are optimized for crowd workers.
proposals.method: We will select 3-4 linguistically complex tasks (e.g., semantic role labeling on Universal Proposition Bank, dialogue act tagging, named entity recognition in code-switched speech). For each, we will collect parallel datasets: one labeled by domain experts and another via a carefully designed, gamified crowdsourcing platform. We will train identical, pre-2026 multilingual transformer architectures (e.g., XLM-R or mT5) on each dataset, using established training protocols and string-based data augmentation. Model alignment and robustness will be further assessed using a prospect-theoretic optimization framework (inspired by KTO) to understand how different label sources affect reward model training and downstream policy performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, empirical benchmark of non-expert vs. expert label quality across a spectrum of language tasks, offering concrete guidelines for when crowdsourcing is sufficient. It will contribute a novel, publicly released multilingual dataset with parallel expert/non-expert annotations, enabling future research on annotation efficiency and model generalization.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses crowdsourcing for sentiment or simple classification, this proposal rigorously tests its limits on tasks requiring deeper linguistic structure, and innovatively connects label source analysis to contemporary model alignment techniques, moving beyond simple accuracy comparisons.

proposals.title: Crowd-Sourced Alignment: Scaling Cross-Lingual and Cultural Annotation for Multilingual Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: The performance of large language and speech models degrades significantly for low-resource languages and non-Western cultural contexts, perpetuating bias and limiting global utility. Pre-2026, annotation efforts are often siloed, expensive, and lack the scale needed for robust multilingual alignment. This creates a critical bottleneck for developing equitable AI systems that serve global populations, a core concern in fairness and accountability research.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, incentive-aware crowdsourcing framework, leveraging global platforms and informed by prospect-theoretic principles of human decision-making, can efficiently produce high-quality, culturally-nuanced annotations for model alignment across a diverse set of languages.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-stage annotation pipeline deployed on global crowd platforms. First, we will adapt the KTO (Kahneman-Tversky Optimization) framework to model annotator behavior and design incentive-compatible tasks for subjective, culturally-grounded judgments. Second, we will use multilingual string matching and embedding-based techniques to create and validate silver-standard prompts for low-resource languages. Finally, we will train and evaluate a suite of compact, multilingual adapter models on this collected data to measure alignment gains.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, large-scale dataset of cross-lingual and multicultural annotations for model alignment, alongside a replicable methodological framework for ethical global data collection. It will advance the technical understanding of how to align foundation models with diverse human values, directly contributing to the fields of multilingual NLP and algorithmic fairness.
proposals.novelty: While crowdsourcing is common, this proposal uniquely integrates behavioral economic models (KTO) directly into the annotation incentive structure and focuses on scaling culturally-situated, rather than merely translated, judgments for alignment—a key gap in pre-2026 multilingual resource development.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Coherent and Controllable Multilingual Dialogue
proposals.motivation: Modern dialogue systems, while fluent, often lack coherent long-term structure and strategic discourse planning, leading to repetitive or aimless conversations. This is especially critical in high-stakes multilingual applications, such as the speech processing for accountability referenced in Jurafsky's recent work, where dialogue coherence and adherence to communicative goals are paramount. A principled approach to modeling discourse-level planning is needed to move beyond next-utterance prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) framework, which separates high-level discourse planning from low-level multilingual utterance generation, will produce dialogues that are more coherent, goal-directed, and controllable than end-to-end fine-tuned models.
proposals.method: We will design a two-level HRL agent. A high-level manager, trained with a sparse reward for task success, will operate over a learned latent space of discourse acts (e.g., inquire, clarify, conclude) to produce a plan. A low-level worker, built upon a pre-trained multilingual language model (e.g., mT5 or XLM-R), will generate the surface text for each act, trained with dense rewards for fluency and semantic alignment. We will adapt the KTO alignment method to optimize the worker's output for both correctness and naturalness, and evaluate on multilingual task-oriented dialogue benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, modular framework for controllable dialogue generation that explicitly models discourse structure. It will provide new insights into how RL can shape dialogue strategy beyond single-turn rewards, and deliver a resource in the form of a trained, analyzable model for studying cross-linguistic discourse patterns.
proposals.novelty: While HRL has been explored in navigation, its application to dialogue with modern foundation models is nascent. The novelty lies in integrating discourse act-based planning with prospect-theoretic alignment (KTO) for multilingual generation, creating a transparent and steerable alternative to monolithic fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Prospect Theoretic Alignment for Low-Resource Task-Oriented Dialogue
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for dialogue policy optimization typically requires vast, costly interaction data, making it infeasible for low-resource domains like endangered language interfaces or specialized professional tools (e.g., accountability software). Current methods, including RLHF and DPO, struggle with sample efficiency and stability when fine-tuning on small, high-quality dialogue corpora. This limits the democratization of robust conversational AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a prospect theory-based alignment objective, like Kullback-Leibler Trust Region Optimization (KTO), will provide more sample-efficient and stable policy learning for dialogue than standard RLHF, by directly optimizing for human preferences without needing explicit pairwise comparisons or reward model training.
proposals.method: We will adapt the KTO framework to task-oriented dialogue. Starting with a multilingual base model (e.g., mT5 or XLM-R), we will fine-tune it on a small, curated corpus of task dialogues (e.g., police stop transcripts). The policy will be optimized using KTO's loss function, which treats chosen (positive) and rejected (negative) utterance completions asymmetrically, aligning with human loss aversion. We will compare against DPO and PPO baselines, evaluating on dialogue success rate and coherence with limited holdout data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical framework for sample-efficient dialogue policy learning, directly applicable to low-resource and multilingual settings. It will provide empirical evidence on the utility of prospect-theoretic alignment beyond general chat, contributing to both NLP methodology and the development of equitable speech technologies.
proposals.novelty: While KTO has been explored for general LM alignment, its application to structured dialogue generation with severe data constraints is novel. The integration of behavioral economic theory with low-resource dialogue RL addresses a core instability in current methods.

proposals.title: Beyond Imitation: A Prospect Theoretic Framework for Aligning Dialogue Agents to Contextual Utility
proposals.motivation: Current dialogue agents are overwhelmingly trained to mimic human conversational patterns, an objective rooted in the Turing Test. However, in high-stakes applications like legal advising, medical triage, or police accountability (themes from Jurafsky's recent work), human-likeness may conflict with goals of clarity, consistency, and fairness. The field lacks a principled framework for defining and optimizing non-anthropomorphic conversational objectives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dialogue agent alignment, when framed as a prospect-theoretic optimization problem (as in KTO) rather than a human-likeness maximization problem, will yield systems with higher utility and lower measured bias in structured, high-stakes domains.
proposals.method: We will develop a multilingual evaluation suite of goal-oriented dialogue tasks (e.g., summarizing police body-cam transcripts, providing standardized legal information) where ideal responses are defined by utility metrics (accuracy, brevity, actionability) rather than human similarity. We will adapt the KTO (Kahneman-Tversky Optimization) algorithm, using these utility metrics as the 'value' function, to train a suite of LLaMA or T5 models. We will compare these against RLHF-aligned baselines, measuring performance on both utility and standard conversational metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a formal, prospect-theoretic framework for moving beyond human-likeness as a singular goal, 2) a new multilingual benchmark for evaluating dialogue utility, and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between anthropomorphism and effectiveness in public-interest NLP applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work critiques anthropomorphism, we propose the first method to directly optimize for a non-human, context-specific utility function using a behavioral economics-based alignment algorithm, explicitly decoupling alignment from imitation.

proposals.title: Beyond WER: A Framework for Application-Specific Semantic Error Scoring to Measure ASR Fairness
proposals.motivation: Current ASR fairness evaluations rely on aggregate Word Error Rate (WER), which treats all errors equally. This fails to capture disparate impact in high-stakes applications like healthcare or law, where a single semantic error (e.g., 'not' vs. 'now') can cause critical harm. A pre-2026 gap exists in metrics that weight errors by their real-world consequences for different speaker groups.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that weighting ASR errors by their application-specific semantic severity will reveal significant, previously obscured disparities in system performance across demographic groups, and that these disparities correlate with downstream task failure rates.
proposals.method: We will deploy multilingual ASR models (e.g., Whisper, wav2vec 2.0) in two domains: clinical dictation and police body-worn camera audio. We will collect and manually annotate error corpora, tagging errors with a severity schema (e.g., clinical risk level). We will then train a BERT-based severity scorer, fine-tuned on these annotations, to automatically weight errors. Finally, we will compute group-disparate impact scores using these weighted error sums instead of raw WER.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) A novel, open-source framework for application-aware ASR fairness evaluation, 2) New annotated error corpora for clinical and legal speech, and 3) Empirical evidence linking semantic error severity to demographic disparities, directly informing fair use and accountability for foundation models.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond post-hoc demographic parity in WER to a causal, task-grounded fairness metric. It integrates Jurafsky's work on semantic consequence in communication with modern multilingual ASR evaluation, creating a resource for harm-weighted assessment.

proposals.title: Beyond WER: A Context-Weighted Error Framework for Fairness in Automatic Speech Recognition
proposals.motivation: Word Error Rate (WER) is the standard metric for evaluating Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems, but it treats all errors as equally important. This is problematic for fairness, as errors on semantically critical words (e.g., 'not', drug names, street names) or in high-stakes domains (e.g., legal proceedings, medical dialogues) can have disproportionate negative impacts on users. A fairness metric that ignores this semantic gravity risks declaring a system 'fair' while it systematically fails vulnerable populations in consequential ways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite error metric, which weights recognition errors by their contextual and semantic importance, will provide a more accurate and actionable measure of ASR fairness across demographic groups and domains than WER alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a Context-Weighted Error Rate (CWER) framework. First, we will create a multilingual resource of high-stakes speech domains (e.g., police interactions, healthcare, customer service) with human-annotated error severity. Second, we will train a contextual importance model, likely a fine-tuned transformer (e.g., BERT, XLM-R), to predict the severity weight of a word or phrase error given the dialogue context and domain. Third, we will integrate these weights into a prospect theory-inspired loss function (extending KTO concepts) to align ASR evaluation with human utility, where missing a critical word is penalized more than missing a filler word.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, open-source benchmarking resource for contextual ASR error analysis; 2) the CWER metric and associated model, offering a more nuanced fairness diagnostic tool; and 3) a methodological bridge between computational linguistics and fairness auditing, demonstrating how semantic analysis is essential for equitable speech technology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted WER's limitations, no pre-2026 framework systematically operationalizes semantic and contextual gravity into a scalable, automated fairness metric for ASR, nor leverages prospect theory to model the disproportionate impact of high-stakes errors.

proposals.title: The Feedback Loop of Bias: A Longitudinal Cohort Study of Behavioral Adaptation to ASR Disparities
proposals.motivation: Research on bias in automatic speech recognition (ASR) has focused on static disparities in performance across demographic groups, attributing them to acoustic or linguistic input differences. This ignores a critical dynamic: users are not passive data sources but active agents who adapt their speech and engagement in response to a system's perceived failures. Understanding this adaptive feedback loop is crucial for developing equitable systems, as user adaptation may inadvertently reinforce or amplify existing biases during model updates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to a biased ASR system induces systematic behavioral adaptations (e.g., hyper-articulation, lexical simplification) within distinct user cohorts, and that these adapted behaviors, when used as training data for subsequent model updates, create a feedback loop that perpetuates or exacerbates performance disparities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a year-long longitudinal study with three cohorts, defined by dialect and L1 background, interacting with a controlled, continuously updated ASR API. The core ASR will be a fine-tuned, multilingual Wav2Vec 2.0 model. We will instrument the pipeline to log detailed behavioral features (e.g., speech rate, pitch range, re-recordings, vocabulary diversity) and system performance (WER). Using causal mediation analysis and time-series modeling (e.g., Vector Autoregression), we will quantify the bidirectional influence between cohort-specific model performance and user behavior. Model updates will be simulated to isolate the effect of adapted input data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical, longitudinal evidence of a behavioral feedback loop in ASR bias, moving beyond static snapshots of disparity. It will yield a novel methodological framework for studying human-AI interaction as a dynamic system and produce concrete metrics for 'adaptation load' that can inform more equitable model training and evaluation paradigms.
proposals.novelty: The study inverts the standard causal direction in ASR fairness research by treating user behavior as a *dependent* variable shaped by system bias and a key *input* variable for future bias, a bidirectional pathway not previously quantified in real-world deployment settings.

proposals.title: Beyond Input Bias: Quantifying User Adaptation and Linguistic Accommodation in Human-ASR Interactions
proposals.motivation: Research on bias in speech and language technologies, such as Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR), predominantly focuses on disparities stemming from acoustic or linguistic input. This framing treats the user as a static source of data, overlooking the dynamic, performative nature of human communication. Understanding how users actively adapt their speech and language when interacting with a known-biased system is crucial for developing truly equitable systems and for legal and policy discussions around algorithmic accountability, as highlighted in recent work on foundation models and fair use.
proposals.hypothesis: When users are made aware of an ASR system's documented demographic performance disparities, they will engage in strategic linguistic and acoustic accommodation, but the efficacy and nature of this adaptation will vary systematically across speaker groups, potentially creating new, second-order inequities.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled, longitudinal user study where participants from diverse dialectal and demographic backgrounds complete transcription tasks with a deliberately degraded ASR model, fine-tuned to exhibit known performance gaps (e.g., higher WER for African American Vernacular English). We will instrument a real-time interface to log acoustic features (pitch, rate, intensity) and linguistic features (lexical choice, syntax) using pre-2026 toolkits (e.g., Praat, spaCy). We will analyze adaptation trajectories using mixed-effects models and string-based similarity metrics to compare user productions against their baseline speech and a 'model-optimized' prompt. The study will be complemented by a qualitative performance analysis of user strategies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for modeling human adaptation to biased AI, shifting the focus from passive disparity measurement to interactive disparity generation. It will yield a novel dataset of adaptive linguistic performances and concrete metrics for 'accommodation effort,' contributing directly to fairer system evaluation and more nuanced debates on user responsibility and developer liability in speech technology pipelines.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard causal direction in bias research by treating the AI system's bias as an independent variable and user behavior as the dependent variable, moving from a static, resource-oriented audit to a dynamic, interactionist model of sociotechnical disparity.

proposals.title: Feedback Loops of Social Perception: A Computational Model of Racialization and Linguistic Feature Entanglement
proposals.motivation: Sociolinguistics has long treated social categories like race as static, confounding variables to be controlled for, rather than as dynamic constructs co-produced through language. This static view fails to explain why specific linguistic features become potent racialized proxies, reinforcing social inequality. A dynamic, computational model is needed to capture the emergent feedback loops between perception and production, a critical gap in pre-2026 sociolinguistic and fairness-in-AI research.
proposals.hypothesis: The racialization of linguistic features emerges from a mutually reinforcing feedback loop: listener expectations, shaped by social stereotypes, amplify minor acoustic or lexical differences, which in turn are adopted or exaggerated by speakers for identity performance, further cementing the perceptual stereotype.
proposals.method: We will build an agent-based simulation where speaker and listener agents interact. Speaker agents produce utterances with variable phonetic features (e.g., /ay/ monophthongization) modeled on real sociophonetic data. Listener agents, initialized with minimal biases, will use a prospect theory-inspired updating mechanism (extending KTO concepts) to reinforce associations between features and social categories based on reward (correct identification). This simulation will be calibrated and validated against longitudinal data from the Corpus of Regional African American Language and controlled matched-guise experiments measuring perception shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated computational theory of linguistic racialization as an emergent, dynamic process. It will yield a novel simulation framework for testing interventions to break harmful perceptual loops and offer concrete insights for developing more equitable speech processing systems by modeling the source of bias, not just its static correlates.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond correlation to model causation in sociolinguistic marking, combining agent-based simulation with prospect-theoretic learning. It treats race not as an input but as an output of a perceptual-linguistic system, a significant departure from standard controlled-variable approaches in the field.

proposals.title: Beyond WER: Semantic Fairness Audits for ASR via Disentangled Dialect Proxies
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established significant racial and dialectal disparities in ASR performance, but the root causes remain entangled, confounding speaker identity, socio-economic proxies, and specific linguistic features. Furthermore, fairness is measured with aggregate Word Error Rate (WER), which treats all errors as equally harmful, ignoring the critical semantic and pragmatic consequences of misrecognizing key words in high-stakes domains like healthcare or law enforcement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) the performance penalty for African American English (AAE) speakers in ASR can be significantly decomposed into distinct, measurable contributions from phonetic, lexical, and prosodic features versus other correlated social factors, and (2) re-weighting errors by their semantic importance will reveal a different, more consequential fairness landscape than WER alone.
proposals.method: We will first create a controlled, multi-factor speech resource using professional linguists and voice actors to systematically vary dialect features (e.g., vowel mergers, grammatical constructions) independent of speaker identity. We then audit state-of-the-art multilingual ASR models (e.g., Whisper, Wav2Vec 2.0) on this corpus and real-world data. For semantic fairness, we will train a BERT-based model to score the contextual importance of words in transcripts from specific domains (e.g., clinical conversations, police interactions), derived from existing semantic role labeling and named entity recognition benchmarks. This will generate a suite of Semantic-Weighted Error Rate (SWER) metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the field with (1) a novel methodology for disentangling sources of ASR bias, (2) a publicly available controlled speech dataset for auditing, and (3) a new, actionable framework for semantic fairness evaluation that moves beyond WER. The outcome will be concrete guidelines for model developers to prioritize mitigation of the most harmful error types.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies disparity, our controlled design isolates linguistic variables. While some propose task-specific metrics, our method generalizes semantic importance scoring via existing NLP models to create a domain-adaptable fairness suite, directly linking sociolinguistics to algorithmic auditing.

proposals.title: Targeted Model Improvement: A Public Health Framework for Reducing Disparities in Multilingual Speech Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Recent advances in large-scale, multilingual foundation models have improved average performance across languages and speaker groups. However, empirical studies (pre-2026) show these gains often fail to close, and can even exacerbate, performance disparities for low-resource languages and marginalized demographic subgroups. This mirrors the 'intervention-generated inequality' problem in public health, where universal interventions widen gaps, creating an urgent need for ML-specific disparity reduction strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the public health principle of 'targeted universalism' to model development—by diagnosing and treating subgroup-specific technical failure modes—will reduce performance disparities more effectively than general optimization for average accuracy, even as overall model quality improves.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework for 'targeted model improvement' in multilingual speech recognition. First, we will audit a pre-trained multilingual model (e.g., Whisper, wav2vec 2.0) to diagnose failure modes (e.g., acoustic feature representation, phoneme coverage) for specific low-resource language subgroups. We will then design and integrate tailored interventions during continued pre-training or fine-tuning, such as subgroup-adaptive sampling of training data, subgroup-specific regularization terms in the loss (inspired by KTO's prospect-theoretic weighting), and targeted data augmentation using available resource-constrained techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, actionable methodology for disparity reduction in model development, moving beyond post-hoc fairness corrections. It will contribute a new diagnostic toolkit for subgroup failure modes in speech models and demonstrate the efficacy of targeted interventions through rigorous evaluation across demographic and linguistic axes.
proposals.novelty: While prior fairness work often focuses on constraints or post-processing, this proposal innovates by directly importing and operationalizing the public health 'targeted universalism' framework into the core training process of foundation models, explicitly linking overall improvement to disparity reduction.

proposals.title: Auditing for Architectural Bias: A Multi-Model Framework for Diagnosing Demographic Disparities in ASR
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that ASR systems exhibit significant performance disparities across demographic groups, particularly for speakers of non-standard dialects and non-native accents. Current auditing practices typically evaluate a single model or a homogeneous set, making it difficult to determine if failures are fundamental to the task or artifacts of a specific model's inductive bias. This limits the development of effective, generalizable mitigation strategies for building inclusive speech technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by auditing a deliberately diverse ensemble of modern ASR architectures (RNN-T, CTC, encoder-decoder) on the same speech data, we can systematically disentangle universal acoustic-linguistic challenges from architecture-specific failure modes, revealing which biases are inherent and which can be mitigated through architectural choice.
proposals.method: We will train or fine-tune strong, publicly available base models for each architecture (e.g., RNN-T from Whisper, wav2vec 2.0 with CTC, and a Transformer encoder-decoder) on a large, multilingual corpus. We will then audit them on carefully curated benchmark suites like CORAAL (for dialectal English) and Common Voice (for accents), measuring Word Error Rate (WER) disparities. The core analysis will involve statistical comparison of performance gaps across the architectural spectrum and error analysis to categorize failure modes as universal or architecture-specific.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel, open-source auditing framework that uses architectural diversity as a diagnostic tool, 2) a benchmark dataset of annotated failure modes linked to model architecture, and 3) concrete guidelines for when and how architectural heterogeneity in deployment can reduce systemic risk for marginalized speaker groups.
proposals.novelty: While model auditing is established, prior work does not systematically vary core ASR architectures to diagnose the source of disparities. Our approach treats architectural inductive bias as a controlled variable to isolate the mechanisms of failure, moving beyond documenting bias to explaining its architectural correlates.

proposals.title: CausalBench: A Minimal-Pair Intervention Framework for Isolating Linguistic Feature Causality in Multilingual Speech Models
proposals.motivation: Current evaluations of multilingual speech and language models (e.g., Whisper, XLS-R) primarily identify correlational performance gaps across languages and dialects. This fails to pinpoint the specific phonological or grammatical features that causally drive errors, hindering targeted debugging and equitable model improvement. Establishing causality is critical for developing accountable systems, especially in high-stakes domains like law enforcement and healthcare where model failures can have severe consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, low-level linguistic features (e.g., vowel length distinctions, tonal contrasts, or specific morphosyntactic agreements) are direct causal factors for increased error rates in automatic speech recognition (ASR) and language models, and that their impact can be isolated through controlled experimental interventions.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel resource: a multilingual 'CausalBench' of minimal pair datasets across typologically diverse languages (e.g., English, Mandarin, Swahili, code-switched speech). Each dataset will contain audio/text pairs where a single linguistic feature is systematically varied (e.g., presence/absence of a grammatical marker) while holding phonetic, lexical, and prosodic context constant. We will then apply targeted causal interventions—using feature-specific perturbation functions and ablation techniques—on the input to pre-trained, multilingual foundation models. The causal effect will be quantified by the Average Treatment Effect (ATE) on Word Error Rate (WER) or perplexity, using methods inspired by causal inference in NLP.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A new methodological framework for causal, rather than correlational, analysis of model failures. 2) Publicly released 'CausalBench' datasets to spur rigorous model auditing. 3) Actionable diagnostics for model developers to prioritize robustness fixes for specific linguistic phenomena, advancing fairness and performance.
proposals.novelty: While minimal pairs are a staple of linguistics, their systematic use for causal intervention in evaluating large, pre-trained multilingual models is novel. This moves beyond descriptive error analysis to a controlled experimental paradigm that isolates the mechanistic impact of individual linguistic features.

proposals.title: Linguistic Vulnerability Maps for Targeted Architectural Diversity in Multilingual Models
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in multilingual NLP, performance remains highly uneven across languages, with systematic failures linked to specific linguistic features like complex morphology or tonal phonology. Current approaches to robustness often rely on scaling single architectures or naive ensemble methods, which can leave shared vulnerabilities unaddressed. This creates a critical need for a principled, linguistically-informed strategy to build systems that are equitable and reliable by design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a portfolio of architecturally diverse models, where each model is explicitly optimized to be robust to a distinct, linguistically-defined failure mode, will yield significantly more robust and equitable multilingual performance than a homogeneous model or an arbitrarily diverse ensemble.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a fine-grained error analysis on a multilingual transformer (e.g., mT5, XLM-R) across a typologically diverse benchmark (e.g., XTREME), creating 'linguistic vulnerability maps' linking high-error phenomena to specific features. Using these maps, we will then train or select a suite of complementary architectures—including a standard transformer, a state-space model (e.g., Mamba) for long-range dependencies, and a hybrid CNN-Transformer for local phonological patterns—each tuned to mitigate a specific vulnerability class. Finally, we will develop a linguistically-aware gating or fusion mechanism to combine these specialized models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel methodology for diagnosing and categorizing model failures using linguistic typology, 2) a framework for 'targeted architectural diversity' that moves beyond monolithic models, and 3) a new, more robust system for multilingual NLP that demonstrably reduces performance gaps across languages.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed model errors or proposed diverse architectures, the key novelty is using deep linguistic analysis as a *guiding signal* to deliberately cultivate and combine architectural variants, creating a synergistic defense against known linguistic vulnerabilities rather than promoting diversity arbitrarily.

proposals.title: CausalMediateASR: Disentangling Racial Bias from Linguistic Variation in Automatic Speech Recognition via Mediation Analysis
proposals.motivation: ASR systems exhibit significant performance disparities across racialized speaker groups, raising urgent concerns about fairness and equitable access. Current auditing methods, like differential testing, can detect these disparities but fail to diagnose whether they stem from problematic bias (e.g., modeling choices that penalize acoustic correlates of race) or legitimate linguistic variation (e.g., systematic phonetic differences). This diagnostic gap hinders the development of targeted interventions and leaves developers without actionable evidence for mitigation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a causal mediation framework, applied to acoustic-phonetic features, can reliably distinguish performance gaps caused by racial bias (where features serving as proxies for racial identity mediate the error disparity) from those caused by linguistically valid variation (where features independent of race mediate the disparity).
proposals.method: We will first apply multilingual, resource-efficient differential testing across a suite of pre-trained ASR models (e.g., Whisper, Wav2Vec2) on corpora like the Corpus of Regional African American Language (CORAAL) and the Speech Accent Archive to identify candidate disparity phrases. For these phrases, we will extract meticulously annotated acoustic-phonetic features (vowel formants, VOT, rhythm metrics) using established toolkits (e.g., Praat). We will then implement causal mediation analysis using a potential outcomes framework, treating speaker group as the treatment, WER as the outcome, and acoustic features as mediators, to estimate the average causal mediation effect (ACME) for each feature.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first diagnostic toolkit that classifies the root causes of ASR disparities, moving the field from detection to explanation. It will provide auditors and developers with interpretable, feature-level evidence to guide debiasing efforts, such as targeted data augmentation or architectural changes. The framework also contributes a novel methodology for fairness research, integrating causal inference with speech processing.
proposals.novelty: While differential testing for ASR fairness is established, the integration of causal mediation analysis to disentangle bias from variation is novel. This approach moves beyond correlational studies to make stronger claims about the mechanisms driving observed disparities, a significant advance over prior work.

proposals.title: Modeling and Synthesizing Demographic-Specific Adversarial Speech via Population Pharmacokinetic Methods
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has documented significant performance disparities in Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems across demographic groups, raising serious fairness and accountability concerns. Existing adversarial example generation methods for speech are often computationally expensive, lack interpretability, and do not provide a principled, model-based framework for systematically controlling the scale of disparity across specific population subgroups.
proposals.hypothesis: That the core mathematical framework of population pharmacokinetic modeling—which predicts a drug's concentration-effect relationship across a heterogeneous population—can be adapted to create a predictive, parametric model of ASR performance, enabling the efficient generation of adversarial speech that precisely targets performance gaps between demographic groups.
proposals.method: We will treat a pre-trained, multilingual ASR model as the 'population.' Using a resource of speech from diverse speakers (e.g., Mozilla Common Voice), we will fit a nonlinear mixed-effects model where demographic covariates (accent, gender, age) influence latent model parameters governing feature perturbation susceptibility. The model will be trained on Word Error Rate (WER) data from adversarial attacks (e.g., projected gradient descent on log-mel spectrograms). We will then invert this fitted model using constrained optimization to synthesize speech perturbations that maximize the predicted WER for a target subgroup while minimizing it for a control group.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, scalable methodology for auditing ASR fairness by generating targeted, model-based adversarial examples. It will contribute a new interpretable framework for understanding how demographic factors modulate a model's adversarial vulnerability, directly informing the development of more robust and equitable speech systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the cross-disciplinary analogy, applying a well-established pharmacological modeling paradigm to the inverse problem in adversarial ML. This moves beyond black-box attack methods to a parametric, covariate-driven model of system performance, enabling controlled disparity scaling.

proposals.title: Quantifying Devaluation: A Multilingual NLP Framework for Detecting Sentiment Bias in Scientific Peer Review and Citation
proposals.motivation: Prior work in the sociology of science suggests that novel, interdisciplinary, or high-risk research faces systematic devaluation in peer review and citation networks, potentially slowing scientific progress. However, quantifying this implicit bias at scale has been challenging. Pre-2026 NLP methods now allow for large-scale, fine-grained sentiment and stance analysis across diverse linguistic contexts, offering a new lens on this critical issue of scientific equity and performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that novel scientific contributions, as measured by bibliometric novelty scores, receive systematically more negative sentiment and hedging language in peer reviews and citing sentences compared to more conventional work, and that this devaluation sentiment varies across linguistic and disciplinary communities.
proposals.method: We will construct a multilingual corpus of peer reviews (from open platforms like OpenReview) and citation contexts (from S2ORC). Using a resource-efficient approach, we will fine-tune a pre-trained multilingual transformer (e.g., XLM-RoBERTa) for a novel multi-task objective: jointly predicting (a) fine-grained sentiment/hedging spans and (b) a devaluation score, using weak supervision from bibliometric novelty indicators. We will train these models on known high-novelty vs. conventional paper pairs, leveraging string-matching techniques to align review and citation text with paper abstracts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, multilingual dataset and model for detecting devaluation sentiment in scientific discourse. It will contribute a new, NLP-based methodological framework to the science of science, offering concrete metrics to audit peer review and citation practices for bias. The trained models could serve as a resource for developing fairer evaluation pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While sentiment analysis is well-studied, its application to quantify implicit bias against scientific novelty is novel. The method's integration of prospect-theoretic alignment (inspired by KTO) to model reviewer risk-aversion, combined with a multilingual, resource-efficient approach to a domain-specific fairness problem, moves beyond standard sentiment classification.

proposals.title: Carbon-Aware Model Cards: Extending Energy Reporting Frameworks to Multilingual NLP and Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: The computational and environmental cost of training large-scale models is a critical, pre-2026 concern, but standardized reporting is nascent. Prior frameworks for energy and carbon accounting, like those emerging for reinforcement learning, have not been systematically adapted to the dominant paradigms of NLP and computer vision. This gap hinders meaningful comparison of the sustainability of different architectures, training strategies, and multilingual deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending a unified energy and carbon reporting framework to NLP and vision tasks will reveal significant, previously unquantified disparities in the environmental impact of (1) monolingual vs. multilingual model training and (2) contrastive pre-training (e.g., CLIP) vs. generative pre-training (e.g., diffusion models) in computer vision.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the 'experiment-impact-tracker' framework to log detailed hardware utilization (GPU/TPU hours, power draw) during training and inference for selected model families. For NLP, we will benchmark a suite of transformer-based models (e.g., BERT, mBERT, XLM-R) on standardized tasks across languages. For vision, we will compare contrastive (CLIP-style) and generative (DALL-E/stable diffusion-style) pre-training pipelines. We will integrate these metrics into an expanded 'Model Card' template, creating 'Carbon-Aware Model Cards' that report performance-per-watt and estimated CO2e alongside traditional accuracy metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, comparative analysis of the carbon costs of major NLP and vision model paradigms. It will deliver a practical, open-source tool for researchers to generate Carbon-Aware Model Cards, promoting sustainability as a core evaluation criterion. The findings will inform more efficient model development and deployment choices in academia and industry.
proposals.novelty: While carbon accounting tools exist, their application has been generic or focused on RL. This proposal novelly tailors the framework to the specific training dynamics and evaluation benchmarks of multilingual NLP and modern vision-language models, enabling domain-specific insights and actionable reporting standards.

proposals.title: The Specialist's Edge: A Framework for Domain-Specialized Foundation Models via Ecological Analogy
proposals.motivation: Current NLP is dominated by the paradigm of training massive, general-purpose foundation models (FMs) on broad web corpora, which are then adapted to specific domains. This approach is computationally expensive, can produce brittle or biased outputs in high-stakes domains, and may be suboptimal for stable, information-rich fields like medicine or law. Drawing from the specialist-generalist trade-off in evolutionary ecology, we hypothesize that a portfolio of smaller, domain-specialized models could offer superior performance and efficiency within their niches.
proposals.hypothesis: For stable, information-rich domains, a foundation model pre-trained exclusively on that domain's corpus will achieve higher downstream task performance, greater sample efficiency for fine-tuning, and more interpretable representations within its niche than a generalist FM of comparable scale.
proposals.method: We will construct a framework of domain-specialized models (e.g., for clinical notes, legal opinions) using standard decoder-only Transformer architectures, pre-trained from scratch on curated domain corpora. We will develop a lightweight, multilingual domain classifier (using string and embedding-based features) as a selection mechanism to route queries to the appropriate specialist. Performance will be benchmarked against a generalist FM (e.g., a comparable-sized LLaMA variant) on domain-specific tasks (e.g., medical QA, legal entailment), measuring accuracy, few-shot learning efficiency, and representation analysis via probing classifiers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, empirically tested framework for building and deploying domain-specialized foundation models, challenging the one-model-fits-all paradigm. It will contribute new insights into the relationship between pre-training data distribution and model capability, with practical implications for creating efficient, trustworthy AI in high-stakes professional domains.
proposals.novelty: While domain-adaptive pre-training exists, the systematic formulation of multiple, narrow FMs as an 'ecological portfolio' with a dedicated selector is novel. It directly tests the ecological analogy's core trade-off in model development, moving beyond simple continued pre-tuning of a generalist base.

proposals.title: Benchmark-Driven Data Curation: A Pre-training Paradigm for Equitable Domain-Specific Automatic Speech Recognition
proposals.motivation: Current ASR systems, pre-trained on vast, unfiltered web data, exhibit significant performance disparities across speaker demographics, especially in high-stakes domains like healthcare and legal settings. This fairness failure can lead to real-world harm and erodes trust in automated systems. The dominant paradigm of scaling general pre-training data has not solved this equity problem, suggesting a need for a fundamentally different approach to data selection.
proposals.hypothesis: Pre-training an ASR model exclusively on a demographically balanced, domain-specific corpus, curated and validated using a standardized fairness benchmark suite, will yield superior and more equitable downstream task performance within that domain compared to models pre-trained on orders-of-magnitude larger, general-purpose data.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-domain fairness benchmark suite, extending concepts from the 'Developing Speech Processing Pipelines for Police Accountability' work. For a target domain (e.g., medical dialogues), we will curate a pre-training corpus by strategically sampling from existing multilingual resources (e.g., MLS, VoxPopuli) and targeted new collection to ensure demographic balance across relevant attributes (accent, gender, age). We will pre-train a transformer-based encoder (e.g., wav2vec 2.0 or HuBERT) from scratch on this curated data and comparatively fine-tune it against a model pre-trained on LibriLight/960. Performance and fairness gaps will be rigorously evaluated on the benchmark. We will analyze the correlation between specific data characteristics (e.g., speaker diversity ratios, lexical coverage) and benchmark metrics to identify causal factors for equity gains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, benchmark-driven methodology for curating equitable pre-training data, 2) empirical evidence on the efficacy of narrow, high-quality data versus broad, low-quality data for domain-specific fairness, and 3) a publicly released benchmark suite and curated data selection guidelines to spur research into equitable pre-training.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard scaling law paradigm by using fairness benchmarks as a design tool for pre-training, not just a post-hoc evaluation. It moves beyond post-processing or fine-tuning fixes to address demographic disparities at the foundational pre-training stage through principled data curation.

proposals.title: Federated Auditory Priming: A Cognitive Feedback Loop for Debiasing Multilingual ASR Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Large ASR foundation models, trained on massive, aggregated datasets, often encode and amplify societal biases, leading to inequitable performance across dialects and speaker groups. This is a critical fairness issue with real-world consequences, as seen in documented performance gaps for speakers of African American Vernacular English (AAVE) and other non-dominant varieties. Current debiasing methods often rely on static, centrally curated fairness metrics that may not capture the dynamic, context-sensitive nature of perceptual bias as understood in psycholinguistics.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating decentralized, localized psycholinguistic priming experiments into a federated learning framework will generate behavioral evidence of model bias that can dynamically steer training, resulting in foundation model parameters that are inherently more equitable across dialects as measured by cognitive priming tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend a federated learning setup (e.g., based on FedAvg) where client nodes represent distinct dialect communities. Each node will run a localized auditory priming experiment: participants hear a biasing context (e.g., a sentence from the global model), followed by an ambiguous phoneme target from the local dialect; their identification responses quantify the model's contextual bias. This bias probe score will be used to re-weight the node's contribution during federated aggregation or to construct a local adversarial loss term, creating a feedback loop. We will implement this using a large-scale multilingual ASR model (e.g., Whisper or Wav2Vec 2.0) as the base architecture.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce 1) a novel federated learning algorithm that integrates behavioral experimentation directly into model optimization, and 2) a new evaluation paradigm for ASR fairness based on psycholinguistic priming, moving beyond word error rate (WER) disparities. It will demonstrate a path toward foundation models whose training is guided by cognitive evidence of bias.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its fusion of federated learning with real-time, in-situ psycholinguistic experimentation to debias models, moving beyond post-hoc audits or static fairness constraints. It operationalizes a cognitive theory of bias (priming) as a continuous training signal.

proposals.title: Error Landscapes as a Scaling Probe: A Theoretical Framework for Emergent Sociolinguistic Capabilities in ASR
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has documented severe performance disparities in ASR systems across dialects, treating bias as a fairness failure. Concurrently, scaling laws in large language models demonstrate emergent capabilities not present in smaller models. This project reframes ASR bias not just as a problem, but as a unique empirical signal to probe the theoretical conditions under which models acquire deep, structured sociolinguistic knowledge, a critical gap in understanding foundation model capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: The dialect-dependent error landscape of an ASR system, when analyzed as a function of model scale and training data composition, exhibits phase transitions that signal the emergence of latent sociolinguistic concepts, enabling the prediction of scaling laws for dialectal reasoning.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-dialect benchmark (e.g., extending the 'Developing Speech Processing Pipelines for Police Accountability' corpus) and measure Word Error Rate (WER) landscapes across systematically varied model scales (parameters/compute) and architectures (e.g., wav2vec 2.0, Whisper). We will apply prospect-theoretic analysis from KTO to model error perception and use multilingual transfer techniques to analyze training data influence. The core analysis will fit scaling laws to error differentials, seeking inflection points where error disparities collapse or reconfigure, indicating capability emergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel theoretical framework linking bias error patterns to emergent capability theory, 2) empirical scaling laws for sociolinguistic reasoning in speech models, and 3) a diagnostic methodology to audit for latent linguistic knowledge, advancing both fairness research and fundamental language modeling theory.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the perspective on bias from a mere performance metric to a core theoretical probe for emergence, and formally connects sociolinguistics with scaling law research in a way pre-2026 literature has not attempted.

proposals.title: Architectural Audits: Diagnosing Sociolinguistic Conflation in Diverse Language Model Families
proposals.motivation: Large language models often encode and reproduce harmful social biases, such as conflating racial identity with dialectal features, which can lead to discriminatory performance in applications like speech recognition or text generation. Existing bias mitigation strategies, like data filtering or post-hoc debiasing, treat the model as a monolithic black box and may not address the root representational causes. A more fundamental, architectural-level understanding of how these biases are learned is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the propensity for a language model to conflate social attributes (e.g., race as a proxy for dialect) is not uniform but is systematically influenced by its architectural inductive biases. By analyzing a suite of models with varied architectures trained on identical sociolinguistic data, we can identify specific architectural components that promote or inhibit the disentanglement of these features.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled multilingual/multi-dialectal corpus with speaker demographic metadata. We will then train or analyze a diverse set of pre-2026 model architectures (e.g., Transformer variants with different attention mechanisms like Linformer or BigBird, modular models like Mixture-of-Experts, and models with different training objectives like ELECTRA or BART) on this corpus. Using probing classifiers and causal mediation analysis, we will measure the degree to which each architecture's internal representations entangle dialectal features with social attributes. The variance across architectures serves as a diagnostic tool.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel diagnostic framework for auditing sociolinguistic bias at the architectural level, moving beyond model-agnostic fixes, and 2) concrete evidence linking specific architectural choices to fairness outcomes, providing actionable guidance for designing less biased models. It directly advances the interpretability and accountability of foundation models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied bias in single models, our approach uniquely uses architectural diversity as a controlled experimental variable to isolate the mechanisms of bias formation. This transforms architectural choice from an engineering decision into a tool for scientific discovery in sociolinguistic fairness.

proposals.title: Cross-Linguistic and Diachronic Validation of Zipf's and Menzerath-Altmann Laws in Large-Scale Multilingual Corpora
proposals.motivation: Statistical laws like Zipf's law and the Menzerath-Altmann law are foundational to quantitative linguistics, but their validation has been piecemeal, often limited to major languages and modern texts. A systematic, large-scale test across diverse language families and historical periods is crucial to determine if these are true linguistic universals or artifacts of specific data domains. This work directly addresses a core question in language modeling and speech processing: the fundamental statistical structure of human language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while Zipf's law of word frequency and the Menzerath-Altmann law (relating constituent size to construct size) will show strong cross-linguistic validity, their precise parameters will systematically vary with linguistic typology (e.g., morphological complexity) and corpus genre, revealing deeper constraints on language performance.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing multilingual and diachronic text corpora (e.g., Wikipedia dumps, OpenSubtitles, the Helsinki Corpus of Historical Texts) and speech transcripts. Using efficient string processing and tokenization pipelines tailored for agglutinative and analytic languages, we will extract frequency distributions and syntactic constituency structures. We will fit power-law and related functions using robust statistical packages, then train a multilingual transformer-based model (e.g., XLM-R) to predict law parameters from typological features and metadata, treating the validation as a resource-driven probing task.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the most comprehensive empirical map of these statistical laws to date, identifying their limits and systematic variations. It will contribute a new benchmark dataset for evaluating the linguistic grounding of foundation models and offer insights for improving multilingual and cross-domain language model training and speech synthesis systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the scale and systematicity of the validation—moving from isolated case studies to a controlled, predictive analysis across hundreds of languages and centuries—and in framing the problem as a model alignment task, using a trained model to discover the features that predict adherence to fundamental laws.

proposals.title: A Unified Diachronic Framework: Joint Modeling of Syntactic, Phonetic, and Semantic Change in Multilingual Speech and Text
proposals.motivation: Historical linguistics has long studied how sound, structure, and meaning evolve, but computational models treat these changes in isolation. This fragmentation limits our ability to model language evolution holistically, especially for under-resourced languages and speech-based historical corpora where changes are interdependent. A unified framework is crucial for applications in digital humanities, forensic linguistics, and understanding the drivers of language change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that syntactic and phonetic changes provide predictive signals for semantic change, and that a joint model of these three dimensions will outperform isolated models in reconstructing historical language states and predicting future change.
proposals.method: We will extend existing multilingual semantic change detection models (e.g., based on BERT or XLM-R) by incorporating two parallel, trained modules: 1) a syntactic change module using multilingual dependency parsing trees and graph neural networks to model structural shifts, and 2) a phonetic change module using forced-aligned speech features from pre-2026 pipelines (e.g., wav2vec 2.0) or orthographic phonetic embeddings for text. These modules will be integrated via a contrastive or prospect-theoretic (KTO-like) alignment objective that learns shared representations of diachronic pressure across modalities.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first resource for joint syntactic-phonetic-semantic change analysis, applicable to both text and speech corpora. It will advance the theoretical understanding of correlated language change and provide a practical tool for historical linguists and sociolinguists, particularly in under-studied language contexts.
proposals.novelty: While semantic change detection is established, integrating real phonetic data (from speech) and syntactic structure into a single, trainable multilingual framework is novel. The use of prospect-theoretic optimization for aligning disparate diachronic signals represents a new methodological approach to the problem.

proposals.title: DiaBench: A Multilingual, Multi-Period Benchmark for Grounded Evaluation of Semantic Change Detection
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of semantic change detection (SCD) models relies on sparse, often monolingual, and inconsistently constructed historical datasets, limiting robust comparison and progress. This is critical for applications in computational social science, historical linguistics, and legal contexts (e.g., interpreting historical statutes), where understanding meaning shift is essential. The lack of a comprehensive, multi-faceted benchmark hinders the reliable assessment of models trained on large multilingual corpora.
proposals.hypothesis: That a systematically constructed, multi-layered benchmark with diverse ground-truth evidence (e.g., expert annotations, parallel texts, dictionary definitions) will reveal significant performance gaps in current SCD methods and enable more reliable model selection and development.
proposals.method: We will construct DiaBench by mining and aligning existing historical linguistic resources (e.g., the Oxford English Dictionary Historical Thesaurus, multilingual etymological databases) and parallel text corpora across multiple time slices (e.g., 1800s, 1900s, 2000s). For a core set of high-frequency words in English, Spanish, and German, we will create gold-standard labels for semantic change type and degree, integrating evidence from glosses, translation pairs, and usage examples. We will then evaluate a suite of contemporary SCD methods—including static (e.g., PPMI, SGNS) and contextual (e.g., BERT, XLM-R) embedding alignment techniques—against this benchmark, analyzing performance variation by language, period, and word type.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a standardized, open-source benchmark for rigorous SCD evaluation, moving beyond single-language, binary change tasks. It will establish clearer best practices for model evaluation and offer diagnostic insights into which architectures and training data (e.g., multilingual vs. monolingual) best capture different types of semantic shift.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates benchmarks for specific languages or tasks, DiaBench integrates multiple evidence sources and languages into a unified framework, enabling controlled cross-lingual and diachronic analysis. This directly addresses the evaluation gap for the multilingual, resource-trained models now prevalent in the field.

proposals.title: Modeling Semantic Change as an Epidemic: A Multilingual SIR Framework for Lexical Diffusion
proposals.motivation: Understanding how new word meanings spread is a core challenge in historical linguistics and sociolinguistics, with implications for tracking misinformation and cultural shifts. Current computational models often rely on static vector spaces or simple frequency counts, lacking a principled, dynamic framework to model the temporal adoption and competition between semantic variants across populations. Pre-2026, epidemiological models have been successfully applied to information diffusion on social networks, but not rigorously adapted to the specific dynamics of semantic change within language systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The adoption and decline of competing word senses within a speech community follows dynamics analogous to disease transmission, such that a Susceptible-Infectious-Recovered (SIR) model, parameterized by linguistic and social variables, can accurately forecast the temporal trajectory of semantic shift. We hypothesize that the 'transmission rate' correlates with factors like word frequency and multilingual contact.
proposals.method: We will operationalize the SIR model by treating a population of language users as susceptible to a new word sense. Using diachronic word embeddings (e.g., from COHA, Google Books Ngrams) for multiple languages, we will quantify the 'prevalence' of a sense as its contextual similarity to a target meaning over time. The model parameters (contact rate β, recovery rate γ) will be estimated via curve-fitting to this prevalence data. We will extend the base SIR framework with multilingual contact layers, where cross-lingual semantic similarity (using aligned multilingual embeddings like LASER) acts as a coupling term, modeling sense transmission across languages. Training will involve optimizing parameters on historical data for known semantic shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative, and predictive framework for semantic change, moving beyond descriptive trends to a testable model of diffusion. It will contribute a new, interpretable method to computational historical linguistics and sociolinguistics, and offer insights into how multilingual contexts accelerate or dampen semantic epidemics, directly extending research on language contact and change.
proposals.novelty: While SIR models have been used for memes or hashtags, applying them to the *semantic content* of words—internal linguistic representations rather than surface forms—and formally integrating multilingual contact as a transmission mechanism is a novel synthesis. This bridges epidemiological modeling with core linguistic theory on semantic fields and change.

proposals.title: Dynamic Meaning Graphs: A Network Science Approach to Polysemy and Semantic Change
proposals.motivation: Understanding how word meanings evolve and exhibit polysemy is a core challenge in computational linguistics and sociolinguistics, with direct implications for robust NLP and historical analysis. Pre-2026 methods often rely on static vector space models or diachronic word embeddings, which can struggle to explicitly model the complex, multi-relational structure of senses and their changes over time. This project addresses the need for a more structured, interpretable framework to capture semantic dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: Polysemy and semantic change can be effectively modeled as dynamic processes on a graph of word senses, where community detection algorithms can identify stable and emerging sense clusters, and graph-theoretic measures can quantify and predict pathways of semantic shift.
proposals.method: We will construct dynamic co-occurrence-based semantic networks from large, time-sliced multilingual corpora (e.g., Google Books N-grams, Common Crawl). Nodes represent word forms and their contextual embeddings (from pre-trained multilingual models like mBERT or XLM-R), with edges weighted by semantic similarity. We will apply multi-resolution community detection algorithms (e.g., the Louvain method or Infomap) to partition the graph into sense communities at each time slice. Temporal dynamics will be analyzed using tools from dynamic graph theory to track community birth, death, merger, and split events, linking these to sociocultural drivers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, graph-based formalism for modeling lexical semantics as a dynamic system, offering more interpretable and structurally rich representations than continuous vector averages. It will contribute a scalable, data-driven methodology for tracking semantic change and polysemy patterns across languages, with potential applications in lexicography, historical linguistics, and bias detection in language models.
proposals.novelty: While network science has been applied to static semantic networks, its systematic integration with dynamic graph theory and modern multilingual embeddings for diachronic sense modeling is novel. This moves beyond tracking single word vectors to analyzing the structural evolution of entire sense ecosystems.

proposals.title: Multilingual Historical Stereotype Dynamics: A Cross-Cultural Embedding Analysis
proposals.motivation: Research on historical stereotypes using word embeddings has been predominantly English-centric, limiting our understanding of how bias evolves in different linguistic and cultural contexts. This gap is critical, as stereotypes are culturally specific and their trajectories can reveal unique social histories. Expanding this analysis globally, using pre-2026 multilingual NLP resources, is essential for a comparative science of societal bias.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diachronic trajectories of social group stereotypes (e.g., based on gender, ethnicity, religion) in historical text embeddings will show culturally distinct patterns of emergence, peak, and decline across languages, reflecting divergent historical events and social movements.
proposals.method: We will apply and extend the diachronic embedding framework (e.g., Hamilton et al., 2016; Garg et al., 2018) to large-scale historical corpora in at least three languages (e.g., Spanish, Mandarin, Arabic), leveraging pre-trained multilingual models (e.g., mBERT, XLM-R) for alignment. Using the 'word intrusion' and 'analogy' tests adapted for cross-lingual analysis, we will quantify stereotype strength over time. Statistical models will correlate these trajectories with historical event data from each culture.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic, quantitative comparison of stereotype evolution across major world languages, moving beyond Anglophone bias studies. It will contribute a novel, open-source pipeline for multilingual historical text analysis and provide empirical evidence on how cultural context shapes representational bias in language.
proposals.novelty: While diachronic embedding analysis exists, its application to a controlled, comparative study of stereotype dynamics across distinct languages and cultures is novel. The project uniquely integrates pre-2026 multilingual foundation models with historical sociolinguistic inquiry to test culturally specific hypotheses.

proposals.title: Causal Pathways in Language: Estimating Media Influence on Stereotype Dynamics Using Instrumental Variable and Multilingual Transformer Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale correlational studies using language models have documented the persistence of stereotypes in text, but they cannot establish the direction of influence between media narratives and societal attitudes. This causal gap limits our ability to design effective interventions for bias mitigation or to understand the societal impact of generative AI. Addressing this is crucial for both computational social science and responsible AI development.
