proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exogenous shocks to media discourse (e.g., major political events, new legislation) can serve as instrumental variables to identify a causal effect of media framing on the evolution of stereotype-associated language in downstream public text, and that this effect varies predictably across multilingual contexts.
proposals.method: We will construct a longitudinal, multilingual corpus of news media and social media/text from 2010-2025. Using techniques from econometrics, we will identify plausible exogenous instruments (e.g., the passage of specific laws) for media framing. We will then train a causal model, building on recent advances in estimating treatment effects with high-dimensional controls, where the 'treatment' is a quantified media narrative (extracted using fine-tuned multilingual transformers like XLM-R) and the outcome is the prevalence and sentiment of stereotype-related language in public discourse, measured via contextual embedding spaces. The analysis will control for confounders using time-series and demographic data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first formal causal frameworks for modeling text-to-society influence, moving the field beyond correlation. It will deliver a novel, reusable methodology for causal inference with textual treatments and outcomes, and offer empirical evidence on the mechanisms of stereotype reinforcement, with implications for content policy and AI alignment.
proposals.novelty: While instrumental variables are established in econometrics, their application to identify causal effects *between* high-dimensional textual representations is novel. Combining this with a multilingual, longitudinal design to test the generalizability of causal pathways addresses a key gap in existing computational sociolinguistics.

proposals.title: Dynamic Network Models of Stereotype Structure in Multilingual Language Models
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that large language models encode and amplify social stereotypes, posing risks for their deployment in multilingual contexts. Current bias detection methods often rely on static word lists or pairwise associations, failing to capture the complex, evolving structure of stereotypes as interconnected clusters of concepts. A network science approach is needed to model how stereotypes form, persist, and potentially dissolve within the semantic space of trained models, directly addressing fairness concerns in speech and language technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: Stereotypes in language models can be modeled as densely connected, persistent communities within dynamic association networks, and targeted interventions on network edges (associations) can induce measurable cluster dissolution without catastrophic model unlearning.
proposals.method: We will construct dynamic semantic networks from co-occurrence and embedding similarity data across multiple languages, using pre-trained multilingual models (e.g., mBERT, XLM-R). Nodes represent concepts (e.g., occupations, identities), and weighted edges represent association strengths derived from model outputs. We will apply community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method, Infomap) to identify stereotype clusters and track their evolution across training steps or fine-tuning interventions. Prospect Theoretic Optimization (KTO) will be adapted to penalize the reinforcement of high-weight edges within identified stereotypical communities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, structural framework for diagnosing stereotype entanglement in language models, moving beyond scalar bias scores. It will yield a new method for network-targeted debiasing and contribute to the theoretical understanding of how social biases crystallize during the training of foundation models, with implications for resource development and audit pipelines in sensitive domains.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied to lexical semantics, its application to dynamically model the *structure* of stereotypes within trained multilingual models is novel. The integration of prospect-theoretic alignment objectives to selectively weaken network communities representing stereotypes represents a non-obvious fusion of fairness and network science methodologies.

proposals.title: Temporal Stylometry and Alignment: Tracking Organizational Language Change with Multilingual Embeddings
proposals.motivation: While large language models (LLMs) are trained on vast corpora, their application to understanding the language of specific entities (e.g., corporations, media outlets, public figures) over time remains underexplored. This is crucial for accountability, as seen in research on police communications, and for understanding how organizational narratives evolve. Pre-2026, methods for such fine-grained, longitudinal analysis using modern embeddings are not standardized.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying multilingual embedding models to the longitudinal text output of specific organizations will reveal measurable, interpretable shifts in linguistic style, thematic focus, and semantic alignment with external events or societal values.
proposals.method: We will curate a diachronic text corpus from target entities (e.g., corporate reports, newspaper archives). Using pre-trained multilingual sentence encoders (e.g., SBERT), we will generate dense vector representations for documents. We will analyze temporal trajectories using techniques like dynamic topic modeling (grounded in these embeddings) and measure semantic drift via cosine similarity and centroid movement across time windows. The analysis will be framed through the lens of model alignment, treating the entity's language as a system to be evaluated against a specified prospect (e.g., fairness, transparency).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, data-driven framework for auditing organizational language change, contributing to computational social science and forensic linguistics. It will extend methods from LLM alignment to real-world institutional text, offering new tools for accountability research. The developed resource of entity-specific temporal embeddings will be valuable for follow-on studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling down alignment and embedding analysis from the level of foundation models to the micro-level of individual organizations, creating a bridge between NLP fairness research and longitudinal stylometry. This moves beyond static sentiment or topic analysis to model dynamic semantic alignment.

proposals.title: Counter-Stereotypical Text Selection: A Prospect-Theoretic Framework for Mitigating Bias in Multilingual Embedding Spaces
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) encode and propagate harmful social stereotypes, posing risks in applications from hiring to law enforcement. Current mitigation strategies, like fine-tuning on curated data, are resource-intensive and can degrade model performance. A more efficient, targeted approach is needed to identify naturalistic texts that can steer models away from biased associations without extensive retraining.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing bias as a directional shift in a semantic embedding space, we can algorithmically identify a sparse set of real-world texts that, when used for lightweight continued training, will move a model's internal representations toward a less stereotypical configuration.
proposals.method: We will operationalize the 'dual problem' using a prospect-theoretic loss function, inspired by KTO, to quantify the utility of a text for reducing stereotype strength. We will compute stereotype association vectors (e.g., for gender-occupation) in a multilingual embedding space (e.g., from XLM-R). Candidate texts from diverse corpora will be scored by their projected gradient on reducing these association vectors. The highest-utility texts will be used in a constrained, low-resource fine-tuning protocol, and the resulting shift in bias metrics (e.g., SEAT, CrowS-Pairs) will be evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, resource-efficient framework for bias mitigation that selects, rather than generates, counter-stereotypical content. It will provide empirical evidence on the feasibility of 'embedding steering' for fairness and advance the methodological integration of behavioral economics (prospect theory) with NLP bias measurement.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in treating bias mitigation as an optimal text selection problem using a prospect-theoretic objective, and in its focus on identifying existing texts that act as efficient 'levers' for altering embedding geometry, contrasting with standard data augmentation or full-model alignment techniques.

proposals.title: Inverting the Alignment: Using Language Models Trained on Biased Human Feedback to Detect and Correct Human Judgment Errors
proposals.motivation: Current alignment research assumes models must be corrected toward idealized human preferences, treating human feedback as a gold standard. However, pre-2026 work in computational social science and fairness, including Jurafsky's on speech processing for accountability, shows human judgments (e.g., in legal or hiring contexts) are often systematically biased. This creates a paradox: models trained on such biased data may perpetuate harm, but could they also learn to identify the bias pattern itself?
proposals.hypothesis: Large language models (LLMs), aligned via methods like RLHF or KTO on demonstrably biased human utility data, internalize representations of that bias which can be repurposed. We hypothesize that these models can then be used to flag and computationally correct analogous biases in new human judgments, effectively inverting the alignment direction.
proposals.method: We will train or fine-tune a multilingual LLM (e.g., Llama 2, BLOOM) on a curated corpus of human decisions with known biases (e.g., simulated hiring data with gender skew, or real-world police transcript evaluations). We align it using a prospect-theoretic objective like KTO to explicitly model human utility. Then, we develop a novel inference-time method, likely a resource-efficient string-based prompting or contrastive decoding strategy, to use the model's internalized bias model. This method will generate 'bias-corrected' counterfactual judgments or confidence scores highlighting likely biased elements in new inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for 'diagnostic alignment,' where AI's capture of human imperfection becomes a tool for audit and improvement. It will provide a new technical method for bias detection that leverages, rather than avoids, models trained on flawed data. The research will also offer empirical insights into the nature of bias representations within aligned foundation models.
proposals.novelty: The approach is non-obvious because it fundamentally challenges the goal of alignment—instead of de-biasing the model, it uses the model's learned bias to de-bias the human system. It inverts the standard assumption that the model's alignment target is an ideal to be reached.

proposals.title: Syntactic-Lexical Fusion Networks: A Gated Multimodal Approach for Pragmatic Classification
proposals.motivation: In NLP tasks like stance detection, formality analysis, and speech act classification, both lexical content and syntactic structure provide crucial, complementary signals. However, pre-2026 models often combine these features via simple concatenation or late fusion, failing to model their complex, conditional interactions. This limits performance on nuanced language understanding tasks, particularly in multilingual or low-resource settings where robust feature integration is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a gated, hierarchical neural architecture that dynamically modulates the influence of syntactic parses based on lexical context will outperform standard feature combination methods on pragmatic classification tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a Syntactic-Lexical Fusion Network (SLFN). Lexical features will be extracted from a pre-trained multilingual transformer (e.g., XLM-R). Syntactic dependency parses will be encoded via a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN). A gating mechanism, inspired by Mixture-of-Experts layers, will learn to weight and combine these representations at different hierarchical levels of the transformer's output. We will evaluate on existing multilingual datasets for pragmatic phenomena, comparing against concatenation-based baselines and ablation models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable architecture for fusing discrete syntactic graphs with dense lexical embeddings. It will advance the methodological toolkit for linguistically-informed NLP, offering a path to more robust classifiers for social-scientific applications like the analysis of police body-worn camera audio transcripts. The code and trained models will be released as a public resource.
proposals.novelty: While GCNs for syntax and transformers for lexicon are established, their integration via a learned, hierarchical gating mechanism for pragmatic tasks is novel. This moves beyond treating syntax as a static, additive signal, instead allowing the model to dynamically attend to structural cues based on semantic context.

proposals.title: Cross-Domain Frame Semantic Parsing via Multilingual Transfer and Constrained Generation
proposals.motivation: FrameNet provides a powerful schema for semantic analysis, but its coverage is limited to specific domains, hindering applications in critical areas like legal discourse or public safety. Extending frame semantic parsing to new domains manually is prohibitively expensive. This project addresses the need for scalable, automatic methods to identify and label novel semantic frames in under-resourced domains, directly supporting research in computational social science and forensic linguistics.
proposals.hypothesis: A multilingual transformer model, fine-tuned on FrameNet data and guided by a constrained decoding strategy, can effectively generalize to recognize and label novel semantic frames in unseen domains by leveraging cross-lingual and cross-domain semantic regularities.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a pre-2026 multilingual transformer (e.g., XLM-RoBERTa) on the English FrameNet 1.7 corpus for frame identification and argument role labeling. To handle novel frames, we will implement a two-stage pipeline: first, a sequence-tagging model detects potential frame-evoking targets; second, a constrained text generation model (e.g., using a T5 architecture with a vocabulary restricted to FrameNet elements and novel frame descriptors from the context) proposes candidate frame labels. This will be evaluated on new domains such as police body-worn camera transcripts and multilingual legal texts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a robust, extensible tool for semantic frame analysis in low-resource domains, contributing directly to projects on speech processing for police accountability. It will also advance the methodology for few-shot semantic parsing by integrating sequence labeling with controlled generation, providing a blueprint for adapting structured lexical resources.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on parsing known frames, this proposal uniquely combines multilingual pre-training with constrained generation to hypothesize and label entirely new frames, moving beyond the closed-world assumption of standard FrameNet parsers.

proposals.title: Beyond the Utterance: Discourse-Aware Role Disambiguation for Police Body-Worn Camera Transcripts
proposals.motivation: Accurate role disambiguation (e.g., distinguishing officer from civilian speech) in police body-worn camera audio is critical for accountability and analysis but remains challenging. Pre-2026 models primarily rely on local lexical and acoustic cues, failing on ambiguous pronouns or short utterances where the necessary disambiguating information lies in prior conversational turns or broader situational context. This limits the utility of automated speech processing pipelines for legal and oversight applications.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating structured discourse-level features—including speaker interaction patterns, topic flow, and referent history—into a multilingual transformer-based model will significantly improve role disambiguation accuracy over models using only local sentence features.
proposals.method: We will extend a pre-trained multilingual speech-text model (e.g., Whisper) by adding a context-encoding module. This module will process a rolling window of prior turns using a Graph Attention Network to model speaker interaction dynamics and a long-context transformer to track entity mentions. These discourse representations will be fused with local utterance embeddings via cross-attention. The model will be trained and evaluated on existing multilingual police transcript corpora (e.g., the Oakland Police Footage corpus), using KTO for alignment with human preferences on ambiguous cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a robust, context-aware model for role disambiguation in real-world conversational audio, directly enhancing speech processing pipelines for accountability. It will also contribute a methodological framework for integrating discourse structure into speech-to-text downstream tasks, moving beyond isolated utterance processing.
proposals.novelty: While discourse context is used in pure text NLP, its systematic integration into speech processing pipelines for a high-stakes social domain is novel. The approach uniquely combines graph-based interaction modeling with prospect-theoretic optimization (KTO) for alignment, addressing both structural and behavioral aspects of the disambiguation problem.

proposals.title: Sequence Labeling for Legal Role Recognition: Applying HMMs and CRFs to Police Body-Worn Camera Transcripts
proposals.motivation: Automated analysis of police body-worn camera (BWC) transcripts is crucial for accountability and oversight, but requires identifying who is speaking and their functional role (e.g., officer, civilian, dispatcher). Pre-2026, role labeling has often been treated as simple classification, ignoring the sequential, turn-by-turn dependencies in conversation. This limits accuracy and fails to model the dialogic structure inherent in police-public interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying classic sequence labeling models—specifically Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) and Conditional Random Fields (CRFs)—to the turn-level sequence of a BWC transcript will significantly outperform context-independent classification for speaker role identification, by explicitly modeling conversational structure and role transitions.
proposals.method: We will adapt established POS-tagging pipelines to the role-labeling task. First, we will define a tagset of conversational roles and extract turn-level features (lexical, prosodic from aligned audio, and dialog acts). We will then train and compare two core models: a generative HMM capturing transition and emission probabilities, and a discriminative linear-chain CRF. These will be trained and evaluated on existing, pre-2026 multilingual BWC transcript datasets, benchmarking against a strong BERT-based sequence tagger baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, interpretable baseline for sequential role labeling in legal speech processing, directly contributing to scalable accountability tools. It will also demonstrate the continued value of classic, data-efficient sequence models in the era of large language models, particularly for structured, resource-constrained domains.
proposals.novelty: While HMMs and CRFs are well-known in NLP, their systematic application to the novel domain of legal conversational role labeling, with a direct comparison to modern neural methods in a low-resource, high-stakes context, is unexplored. This bridges classic computational linguistics with contemporary fairness and accountability research.

proposals.title: Active Learning for Semantic Role Labeling: Transferring Query Strategies from Text Categorization
proposals.motivation: Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) is crucial for deep language understanding but requires expensive, expert annotation, especially for low-resource languages and domains. Active learning (AL) can reduce annotation cost, but its application to structured prediction tasks like SRL is under-explored compared to text categorization. This project addresses the resource bottleneck in creating high-quality, multilingual SRL systems, aligning with needs in areas like speech processing for accountability, where understanding 'who did what to whom' is essential.
proposals.hypothesis: Query strategies (e.g., uncertainty sampling, query-by-committee) proven effective for text categorization can be successfully transferred and adapted to guide sentence selection for SRL annotation, leading to more data-efficient model training than random sampling.
proposals.method: We will adapt established text-based AL strategies (least confidence, margin sampling, entropy) to the structured SRL task by defining sentence-level uncertainty as the aggregate of uncertainty over all predicate-argument pairs. Using a pre-2026 multilingual transformer (e.g., XLM-R) as a base SRL model, we will simulate an iterative AL loop on benchmark datasets (e.g., Universal Proposition Bank). We will rigorously compare the data efficiency of transferred strategies against random baseline and task-specific heuristics, measuring performance gains in F1 across annotation budgets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework and empirical results for applying AL to SRL, directly reducing the annotation cost for creating SRL resources. It will yield practical guidelines on the most effective query strategies for structured prediction tasks, contributing to more equitable and efficient development of NLP tools across languages.
proposals.novelty: While AL is well-studied for classification, its direct transfer to the complex, structured SRL task is non-trivial and less explored. The novelty lies in the formal adaptation and evaluation of categorization-derived query strategies to optimize annotation for predicate-argument structure, rather than simple labels.

proposals.title: Controlled Surface Realization via Semantic Role Inversion with Multilingual Language Models
proposals.motivation: Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) is a core NLP task for extracting predicate-argument structures from text, but the inverse problem—generating fluent, varied sentences from these structures—is under-explored. This inverse task is crucial for controllable text generation, data augmentation for low-resource languages, and testing linguistic theories of argument realization. Pre-2026, large language models (LLMs) excel at generation but lack explicit, interpretable control over semantic content.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequence-to-sequence model, explicitly trained to map structured semantic role frames to natural language sentences, can generate more semantically faithful and lexically diverse output than prompting a general-purpose LLM, especially in multilingual and low-resource scenarios.
proposals.method: We will frame the task as conditional text generation. Using pre-2026 SRL resources (e.g., PropBank, Universal Proposition Bank), we will create parallel datasets of (role frame, sentence) pairs across multiple languages. We will fine-tune a multilingual T5 or mT5 model, treating the linearized predicate and role-argument pairs as the source sequence and the target sentence as the target sequence. Training will incorporate a KTO-style alignment objective to prioritize grammatically and pragmatically natural outputs over merely correct ones, and we will evaluate using automatic metrics (BLEU, SARI) and human assessments of fluency and semantic fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, controllable generation model for semantic role structures, a new multilingual benchmark dataset for the task, and insights into the mapping between deep semantic representation and surface form. It contributes directly to research on interpretable AI and low-resource NLP, aligning with themes of fairness and accessibility in language technology.
proposals.novelty: While SRL is mature, inverting it as a controlled generation task using modern multilingual sequence-to-sequence models with alignment objectives is novel. The approach moves beyond simple template filling or prompting, offering a trainable, resource-efficient method for content-grounded text generation.

proposals.title: Calibrating Distant Supervision: A Probabilistic Latent Variable Framework for Noisy and Incomplete Training Labels
proposals.motivation: Distant supervision is a crucial technique for scaling NLP to low-resource domains, such as in speech processing for social science applications, but it introduces significant label noise and incompleteness. Current multilingual and foundation models trained on such data risk encoding and amplifying these systematic biases, undermining fairness and reliability. Explicitly modeling this uncertainty is essential for accountable AI, particularly in high-stakes domains like the legal or law enforcement contexts studied in Jurafsky's prior work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the noise and missingness processes of distant supervision labels as latent variables within the training objective will yield models with better calibrated uncertainty estimates and improved robustness on downstream tasks. This approach will outperform standard training that treats noisy labels as ground truth.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational EM framework where the true, unobserved label is a latent variable, and the distant supervision label is an observed, noisy emission. The noise model will be parameterized, potentially using prospect-theoretic functions from KTO to capture asymmetric label biases. This probabilistic layer will be integrated into the training loop of transformer-based models, allowing gradient-based learning of both the noise parameters and the primary model weights. We will evaluate on multilingual tasks using speech transcript datasets where distant supervision is common.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, trainable method to account for label uncertainty in distantly supervised learning. It will contribute new metrics for model calibration under label noise and release open-source training code adaptable for various NLP and speech tasks. The findings will inform debates on fair use by showing how training data provenance affects model trustworthiness.
proposals.novelty: While noise-aware loss functions exist, integrating a learnable, task-specific latent variable model of the *distant supervision process itself* into modern foundation model training is novel. This moves beyond simple weighting or filtering to probabilistically reason about the origin of training labels.

proposals.title: Prospect-Theoretic Multi-Instance Learning for Robust Cross-Sentence Relation Extraction
proposals.motivation: Relation extraction (RE) from text often requires aggregating evidence from multiple sentences mentioning the same entity pair, a classic multi-instance learning (MIL) problem. Pre-2026, standard MIL pooling methods (e.g., max, mean) are brittle, treating all instances independently and ignoring the complex, often contradictory, linguistic signals across contexts. This limits the reliability of RE systems in high-stakes multilingual applications, such as processing legal or police reports, where nuanced, distributed evidence is common.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-instance relation classifier, which uses a prospect theory-inspired utility model to weight and aggregate sentence-level evidence, will yield more robust and accurate predictions than standard pooling methods, particularly on noisy, multilingual data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the KTO (Kahneman-Tversky Optimization) framework, a prospect-theoretic alignment method, to the MIL setting. Instead of aligning model outputs with human preferences, we will train a neural utility function to score each sentence (instance) based on its perceived 'value' as evidence for a relation, factoring in gains (supportive cues) and losses (contradictory or ambiguous cues). A transformer-based encoder (e.g., XLM-R) will generate instance representations. The final bag-level prediction will be a learned, non-linear aggregation of these utility-weighted representations, moving beyond simple pooling.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, cognitively-inspired MIL algorithm for NLP that directly models the subjective valuation of evidence. It will provide a new, robust toolkit for cross-sentence RE, directly applicable to Dr. Jurafsky's work on speech processing for police accountability and multilingual analysis. We will release trained models and code as a resource for the community.
proposals.novelty: While MIL and prospect theory exist separately, applying a formal model of decision-making under risk (KTO) to dynamically weight linguistic instances for RE is novel. This moves beyond treating instances as independent or using fixed aggregation schemes.

proposals.title: Topic-Aware Relation Extraction: Disambiguating Relations with Latent Document Context
proposals.motivation: Relation extraction (RE) models, especially those based on pre-trained language models, often struggle with polysemous relation mentions where the same surface form indicates different semantic relationships depending on the broader document topic (e.g., 'executive' in a corporate vs. government context). Pre-2026 RE systems primarily rely on local sentence context, neglecting the global thematic coherence of a document, which is a key cue for human disambiguation. This limits performance in real-world, noisy text where relations are expressed across sentences and are topic-dependent.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating latent topic features, derived from the entire document, into a neural relation extraction model will provide crucial disambiguating context, leading to improved accuracy on polysemous and cross-sentence relation mentions.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream neural architecture. The first stream is a standard sentence-level encoder (e.g., BERT) for the relation mention and its immediate context. The second stream uses a neural topic model (e.g., a ProdLDA variant) trained on the document corpus to infer a latent topic distribution for the entire document. The topic vector is then fused with the local contextual representation via a gating mechanism, and the combined representation is used for relation classification. We will train and evaluate this model on established RE datasets like TACRED and DocRED, comparing against baseline models without topic features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interpretable method for injecting document-level thematic information into relation extraction, moving beyond sentence-level modeling. It will yield a publicly released multilingual topic-aware RE model and an analysis of which relation types benefit most from topical context, advancing robustness in information extraction for domains like legal documents and news analysis.
proposals.novelty: While topic models and RE have been studied separately, their tight integration for disambiguation is underexplored. The novelty lies in the specific fusion mechanism that dynamically weights topic vs. local context and the application to disambiguating relations in multilingual, domain-shift scenarios relevant to Jurafsky's work on speech and legal text processing.

proposals.title: Online, Multilingual Alignment for Dynamic Web-Scale Corpora
proposals.motivation: Current large language models are trained in static, expensive batches, making them unable to incorporate new linguistic data, cultural shifts, or factual updates efficiently. This is a critical limitation for applications requiring real-time adaptation, such as monitoring evolving online discourse for accountability or maintaining accurate, current knowledge bases. An online learning paradigm is needed to handle the continuous, dynamic nature of web-scale data streams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an online variant of alignment algorithms like KTO (Kahneman-Tversky Optimization), combined with efficient data streaming and selective replay, can enable stable and effective continual model updating from dynamic corpora while preserving performance on prior tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop an online KTO framework that processes streaming multilingual text and speech transcripts. The core technical approach involves: 1) a reservoir sampling-based data buffer to maintain a representative subset of past data for rehearsal, 2) a gradient-based importance weighting mechanism to prioritize impactful new examples and mitigate catastrophic forgetting, and 3) integration with a dynamic knowledge graph updater to revise factual embeddings. We will test this on a stream of Common Crawl dumps and real-time police body-cam transcript feeds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first scalable method for online alignment of foundation models, moving beyond static fine-tuning. It will deliver a publicly released framework for incremental model updating, with direct applications in maintaining up-to-date NLP systems and longitudinal sociolinguistic analysis of evolving language.
proposals.novelty: While online learning exists for classification, applying it to the complex reward landscapes of modern alignment objectives like KTO for web-scale, multilingual data is novel. The integration of dynamic knowledge base updates within the online learning loop is also an unexplored challenge.

proposals.title: Beyond Sentiment: Testing the Generalizability of Compositional Semantics in Multilingual Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Research on compositional generalization in NLP, particularly for sentiment analysis, has shown models often fail to robustly combine linguistic units. However, it is unknown if these failure patterns extend to logical (e.g., negation, quantification) and factual (e.g., entity-relation-attribute) compositionality, which are critical for reliable model deployment in high-stakes domains like legal or medical applications. This gap limits our understanding of whether current model architectures and training paradigms learn fundamental compositional principles or superficial, task-specific patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the compositional generalization failures observed for sentiment do not fully predict failures for logical and factual composition, revealing distinct and more severe limitations in how foundation models represent and combine meaning.
proposals.method: We will construct a new multilingual benchmark, LOGIFACT, using controlled template generation and human validation, to test compositionality across sentiment, logic, and factual domains. Using a suite of pre-trained multilingual models (e.g., mT5, XLM-R) and string-based prompting methods, we will perform systematic probing and fine-tuning experiments. We will analyze performance gaps using error analysis and representation probing techniques (e.g., CCA) to identify whether failures stem from lexical, syntactic, or semantic misalignments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic comparison of compositional generalization across semantic domains, yielding a new diagnostic benchmark. It will offer actionable insights for improving training objectives and model architectures to achieve more robust semantic understanding, directly informing the development of more reliable speech and language processing pipelines for accountable AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied compositionality in isolation (e.g., for sentiment or negation), this project directly tests the critical but unverified assumption that findings from one semantic domain generalize to others, using a unified multilingual evaluation framework.

proposals.title: Compositional Emergence in Multilingual Transformers: A Cross-Linguistic Scale Analysis
proposals.motivation: Large multilingual models (e.g., mBERT, XLM-R) are widely deployed, yet their internal representations across languages remain poorly understood. This opacity raises critical issues for fairness and performance, as model behaviors may be inconsistent or biased across linguistic groups. A systematic analysis of compositional structure is needed to audit these models and guide more equitable, efficient multilingual training.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multilingual transformer models develop language-agnostic, compositional representations for syntactic and semantic phenomena, but that the degree and consistency of this compositionality vary systematically based on linguistic typology and training data volume.
proposals.method: We will apply and extend recent visualization and probing techniques for scale analysis (e.g., based on Anthropic's 'Monosemanticity' work and linear representation probes) to multilingual encoders. Using a controlled set of parallel syntactic and semantic constructions across typologically diverse languages, we will train sparse autoencoders to identify interpretable features. We will then analyze the cross-lingual alignment and sparsity of these features as a function of model scale and language resource level.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, empirical map of compositional representation emergence across languages in a single model. It will yield actionable insights for diagnosing cross-lingual transfer failures and for data-efficient multilingual training. The findings will also inform legal and policy discussions around foundation model fairness and transparency.
proposals.novelty: While scale analysis has been applied to English models, its systematic application to the multilingual setting to test theories of cross-lingual compositionality is novel. The integration of linguistic typology with mechanistic interpretability methods moves beyond performance-based evaluation to a causal, structural analysis.

ideas.title: Apply bias correction to more NLP tasks

ideas.title: Architectural Diversity for Disentangling Sociolinguistic Features in Language Models
ideas.description: This research proposes that architectural diversity in language models can be strategically employed to study and mitigate the conflation of social attributes. By training or analyzing a suite of models with varied architectures (e.g., different attention mechanisms, modular designs, or training objectives) on the same sociolinguistic data, we can observe which architectures inadvertently learn 'race as a proxy for dialect' and which succeed in disentangling these features. The variance in performance across architectures provides a diagnostic for identifying the specific representational pathways that lead to harmful conflation. The outcome is a dual advance: a method to audit and reduce sociolinguistic bias, and a new, concrete justification for architectural diversity as a mechanism for improving model fairness and interpretability.

ideas.title: Theoretical Emergence of Linguistic Bias: Using ASR Error Landscapes to Probe and Predict Model Sociolinguistic Capabilities
ideas.description: This research proposes that the error patterns of biased Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems, when analyzed across dialects and scaling regimes, serve as a unique empirical dataset and theoretical probe for emergent capabilities in foundation models. By treating the 'error landscape' across dialects as a function of model scale, architecture, and training data, we can develop theoretical frameworks to predict when and how models transition from superficial acoustic pattern matching to the emergence of deep, structured sociolinguistic knowledge. This approach inverts the problem of bias, using it as a diagnostic tool to formalize and quantify the acquisition of latent linguistic concepts, potentially revealing scaling laws for social and dialectal reasoning that are not apparent in standard performance metrics.

ideas.title: Disentangling Bias in ASR: From Dialect Proxies to Semantic Fairness
ideas.description: This research program first designs controlled studies to separate the influence of racial identification, socio-economic background, and specific regional dialect features on ASR performance. It then moves beyond aggregate Word Error Rate (WER) by developing a suite of context-aware fairness metrics that weight recognition errors based on their semantic importance and pragmatic impact (e.g., misrecognizing a medication name vs. a filler word). The synergy lies in using the disentangled linguistic variables to train or audit models specifically for semantic robustness across dialects, ultimately proposing fairness interventions that target the most harmful error types for specific linguistic communities, not just error rates.

ideas.title: Modeling the Emergent Entanglement of Social Perception and Linguistic Production
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond attempting to disentangle static variables and instead models how racialized perception, socio-economic signaling, and dialect features dynamically co-construct one another. It combines computational simulations of stereotype formation with real-world longitudinal data on linguistic behavior and finely controlled perceptual experiments. The goal is to build a robust theoretical model of the feedback loops between social categorization and linguistic practice, explaining why certain features become racialized proxies rather than treating race as a mere confound for dialect.

ideas.title: Benchmark-Driven, Domain-Specialized Pre-training for Fair ASR
ideas.description: This research proposes to invert the paradigm of broad pre-training by using a standardized fairness benchmark suite as a design tool to curate and validate narrow, domain-specific pre-training data. The core investigation is whether pre-training exclusively on a carefully constructed, demographically balanced corpus for a specific domain (e.g., healthcare, legal, or customer service) yields superior and more equitable downstream task performance compared to models pre-trained on vast, unfiltered general data. The resulting methodology would not only produce fairer ASR systems for critical applications but would also use the benchmark metrics to scientifically identify which domain-specific data characteristics most effectively reduce demographic disparities during pre-training itself.

ideas.title: Sentiment may not generalize to all compositionality

ideas.title: Longitudinal Cohort Study of User Behavioral Adaptation to Biased ASR Systems
ideas.description: This research applies an epidemiological cohort study design to longitudinally track distinct user groups as they interact with a continuously updating ASR system. By inverting the assumption that disparities stem only from acoustic or linguistic input, the study specifically measures how user behavior—such as hyper-articulation, vocabulary simplification, or disengagement—adapts over time in response to perceived system bias. This allows for the analysis of how these behavioral adaptations, in turn, feed back into the system's performance metrics and subsequent model updates for each cohort. The core novel insight is quantifying the bidirectional causal pathway: system bias induces behavioral change, and that change then becomes a new, system-shaped input that can perpetuate or amplify disparities.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement Framework for Diagnosing Racial Bias vs. Linguistic Variation in ASR
ideas.description: This research integrates differential testing with causal mediation analysis to move beyond detecting output disparities to diagnosing their root causes in ASR systems. The framework first employs differential testing across multiple ASR models to surface input phrases where word error rates diverge significantly across racialized speaker groups. For these candidate disparities, it then applies a causal analysis using meticulously annotated acoustic-phonetic features (e.g., vowel formants, timing) to test whether the performance gap is mediated by features that are proxies for racial identity (indicating bias) or by linguistically valid features independent of race (indicating legitimate variation). The final output is a diagnostic toolkit that classifies disparities and provides actionable, interpretable evidence for model auditors and developers.

ideas.title: Refine evaluation with more ground truth

ideas.title: Linguistically-Guarded Architectural Diversity for Robust Multilingual NLP
ideas.description: This research proposes to use detailed linguistic analysis of model failure modes—identifying specific phonological, syntactic, or morphological features that cause high error rates—as a guiding signal for designing and training architecturally diverse models. Instead of promoting diversity arbitrarily, the goal is to cultivate a portfolio of models (e.g., transformers, state-space models, hybrid architectures) where each variant is explicitly optimized or selected for robustness against a distinct, linguistically-defined vulnerability class. This creates a synergistic defense: the linguistic analysis provides a targeted roadmap for diversity, while the architectural diversity ensures no single point of failure for any identified linguistic feature, leading to a system that is more robust and equitable across languages by design.

ideas.title: Expert labels are the perfect gold standard
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that expert annotations are always the optimal ground truth for model training.

ideas.title: Decentralized Auditory Priming for Bias-Aware Foundation Model Training
ideas.description: This research proposes a federated learning framework for training large-scale automatic speech recognition (ASR) foundation models where each participating node (e.g., a device or regional server) not only contributes data but also runs localized psycholinguistic priming experiments. These experiments systematically test how the current global model's context biases affect the recognition of phonemes from the local dialect. The results of these priming 'bias probes' are then used to dynamically weight contributions or guide adversarial debiasing during aggregation, creating a feedback loop where the decentralized training process is directly steered by empirical, behavioral evidence of perceptual bias. This enables the development of a foundation model whose architecture and parameters are inherently optimized for equitable performance across dialects, as assessed by the very cognitive phenomena that reveal human-like listener biases.

ideas.title: Targeted Interventions for Disparity Reduction in ML: A Public Health Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that general model improvement automatically narrows performance gaps across demographic groups by drawing an analogy from public health epidemiology. In public health, general population-wide health improvements often fail to close, and can even widen, health disparities because they do not address the specific social determinants and barriers faced by marginalized groups. The field's solution is 'targeted universalism,' which sets a universal goal but employs subgroup-specific interventions. Adapting this, the research proposes that in machine learning, simply optimizing for average accuracy is insufficient for fairness. Instead, we must adopt a framework of 'targeted model improvement.' This involves diagnosing the distinct technical failure modes (e.g., specific feature representations, data sparsity) causing poor performance for particular demographic subgroups and then designing and applying tailored interventions—such as subgroup-specific regularization, adaptive data sampling, or custom loss components—during model development. The goal is to ensure that the process of raising overall model performance is explicitly and mechanistically coupled to the reduction of performance disparities, preventing the common outcome where a better 'average' model still has significant, or even larger, gaps for disadvantaged groups.

ideas.title: Adversarial Speech Generation via Pharmacokinetic Population Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts population pharmacokinetic modeling from pharmacology to the domain of adversarial machine learning in speech. The analogous problem is predicting and controlling a substance's effect across a diverse population; here, the 'substance' is a modified speech signal, and the 'effect' is ASR performance. We construct a population model of the ASR system, where demographic attributes (like accent, gender, age) are covariates that influence model parameters governing feature extraction and decoding. The solution involves fitting this model to performance data across groups, then inverting it to synthesize speech that maximizes the performance gap between target subgroups. This creates a scalable, model-based method for generating demographic-specific adversarial examples, directly addressing the inverse problem of controlled disparity scaling.

ideas.title: Application-Aware Semantic Error Scoring for ASR Fairness
ideas.description: This research develops a methodology to evaluate ASR fairness by moving beyond Word Error Rate (WER) to create application-specific semantic error metrics. It involves first deploying ASR systems in real-world contexts like clinical dictation or legal transcription to collect domain-specific error corpora. These errors are then annotated and weighted based on their potential for harm, miscommunication, or task failure within that specific application. The outcome is a suite of contextual fairness scores that directly measure disparate impact on user groups based on the functional consequences of errors, not just their raw count.

ideas.title: Architecturally Diverse Auditing Frameworks for Inclusive ASR
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel auditing framework that leverages a diverse ensemble of ASR architectures—including RNN-T, CTC, and encoder-decoder models with varied inductive biases—to systematically audit for demographic disparities. By comparing performance differentials across this architectural spectrum for speakers of different dialects, accents, and language backgrounds, the framework can distinguish between universal failure modes and architecture-specific biases. This allows for the creation of more robust, architecture-aware mitigation strategies and provides a concrete benchmark for evaluating whether architectural diversity in deployment actually reduces systemic risk for marginalized speaker groups. The outcome is a dual-purpose tool that both measures inclusivity and guides the development of more resilient, heterogeneous ASR ecosystems.

ideas.title: Causal Intervention Framework for Isolating Linguistic Feature Impact on Model Errors
ideas.description: This research implements an experimental paradigm to move beyond correlation and establish causality. It involves creating minimal pair datasets where specific phonological or grammatical features are systematically varied while holding all other contextual factors constant. By applying targeted interventions—such as feature ablation, perturbation, or controlled introduction—and measuring the resultant change in model error rates, the study can isolate the direct causal contribution of each linguistic feature. This approach yields actionable insights for model debugging and targeted robustness improvements.

ideas.title: Domain-Specific Pre-training via Ecological Specialization Analogy
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from evolutionary ecology's specialist-generalist trade-off, this idea challenges the assumption of broad pre-training. In stable, resource-rich environments, specialist species evolve to outperform generalists. Similarly, in stable, information-rich domains (e.g., medicine, law), a model pre-trained as a 'specialist' on that specific corpus should develop more efficient and effective representations than a generalist model. The adapted research idea is to create a framework of multiple, narrow, domain-specific foundation models and a selection mechanism to match the model to the task's domain, testing the hypothesis that this ecological approach to model specialization yields superior downstream task performance, sample efficiency, and interpretability within its niche compared to a broadly pre-trained generalist.

ideas.title: Disentangled Latent Adversarial Debiasing for ASR with Explicit Feature Preservation
ideas.description: This approach adapts adversarial training to debias ASR representations by first learning a disentangled latent space that separates linguistic content, acoustic conditions, and a residual component. A targeted adversarial network is applied solely to the residual component to scrub demographic signals, preventing the removal of task-critical features. Simultaneously, auxiliary classifiers are used to explicitly reinforce and preserve the linguistic and acoustic subspaces, ensuring core recognition performance is maintained. This results in a more robust and effective debiasing method that mitigates the performance-fairness trade-off inherent in naive adversarial approaches.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacovigilance Cohort Methods for ASR Model Surveillance
ideas.description: This idea adapts the longitudinal cohort study methods from pharmacovigilance and post-marketing drug surveillance to the context of Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR). In pharmacovigilance, cohorts are tracked long-term to monitor drug safety and efficacy in the general population after clinical trials. Analogously, we propose establishing an 'ASR Model Surveillance Registry' with a fixed cohort of users or user groups defined by demographic, acoustic, or linguistic features. This cohort will be actively monitored over time as ASR models receive updates and new versions are deployed. Key performance and fairness metrics will be collected longitudinally, enabling the detection of delayed or emergent performance degradation, 'adverse' outcomes like increased bias against certain groups, and changes in real-world efficacy—effectively providing a continuous performance and safety monitoring system for evolving AI models.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Sampling and Prioritization for Global ASR Audits
ideas.description: Expanding the ASR audit to a global scale presents a massive sampling challenge. Drawing an analogy from conservation biology's approach to biodiversity assessment, this research adapts the method of phylogenetic sampling. In conservation, scientists use evolutionary trees to select a representative subset of species that captures maximum genetic diversity, often prioritizing threatened lineages. Adapted here, the solution involves constructing or leveraging existing linguistic phylogenetic trees of world languages and dialects. The audit would then strategically sample from across this tree, selecting languages and dialects that represent major branches and typological features (e.g., tonal vs. non-tonal, different phonemic inventories). This approach is combined with a prioritization framework that focuses resources on auditing low-resource ('threatened') languages and high-impact, widely spoken varieties. The result is a scalable, systematic, and efficient global audit methodology that provides a comprehensive picture of ASR performance across the planet's linguistic diversity without requiring impossibly exhaustive testing.

ideas.title: Analyze specific linguistic features causing errors

ideas.title: Diagnostic & Community-Driven Audit for Under-Resourced Language Families in ASR
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a broad survey to conduct a deep, diagnostic audit focused on selected under-resourced language families. It partners with linguistic communities and field linguists to create novel, high-fidelity test datasets that capture phonemic, morphological, and pragmatic challenges absent from standard benchmarks. The system employs a controlled diagnostic framework to isolate specific ASR failure modes, providing actionable insights for model improvement. The outcome is a rigorous, scalable methodology for auditing ASR equity, prioritizing depth of understanding for critical gaps over superficial global coverage.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map ASR Bias Landscapes
ideas.description: This idea adapts Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) from biogeography to the problem of finding inputs where Automated Speech Recognition (ASR) systems produce racially disparate outputs. In ecology, ENM identifies the environmental conditions correlating with a species' presence to predict its full geographic range. Analogously, we treat a corpus of audio inputs known to trigger racially biased transcriptions as 'presence points' within a multidimensional feature space encompassing phonetic, prosodic, syntactic, and speaker attribute data. Using ENM algorithms (e.g., Maximum Entropy modeling), we learn the complex combination of features that defines the 'niche' for biased outputs. This model can then systematically probe the ASR system, scoring any potential input for its likelihood of revealing disparity, thereby guiding differential testing toward the most vulnerable regions of the input landscape and generating novel, high-probability test cases that random fuzzing might miss.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution and Mitigation of ASR Disparities in High-Stakes Contexts
ideas.description: This research moves beyond performance measurement to diagnose the root causes of ASR error disparities in applications like healthcare dictation. It employs a causal framework to decompose errors into constituent factors—acoustic, linguistic, and speaker-based—and uses a controlled perturbation engine to test their individual and interactive effects. The system then evaluates targeted mitigation strategies, such as domain-adaptive training or acoustic conditioning, for each identified causal pathway. The outcome is a set of actionable, factor-specific engineering guidelines to reduce disparities, not just document them.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of Systemic Bias and User Adaptation in Speech Systems
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-arm, longitudinal experiment where participants interact with speech systems that vary along controlled dimensions of acoustic and linguistic bias. Crucially, it includes a counterfactual condition with a demonstrably unbiased system to establish a performance and behavioral baseline. The design measures not only how users adapt to biased systems, but quantifies the causal degradation in outcomes (e.g., task success, cognitive load, trust) attributable solely to the systemic bias, separating this effect from general learning curves. This allows for a direct comparison that frames bias as a source of measurable harm rather than a neutral environmental variable.

ideas.title: Sociolinguistic Auditing for Legally Actionable AI Fairness
ideas.description: This research fuses the matched-guise technique with interdisciplinary governance frameworks to develop a sociolinguistic audit for foundation model ASR systems. It synthesizes controlled speech samples that isolate specific dialect features to produce rigorous, court-admissible evidence of performance disparities. This evidence directly feeds into legal risk assessments, economic models of harm, and ethical audits, moving beyond mere bias detection to creating enforceable accountability standards. The result is a tool for regulators and impacted communities to demand technically precise and legally actionable remedies for linguistic discrimination.

ideas.title: Adaptive User Feedback Loops as a Corrective Mechanism for Biased Speech Systems
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that disparities in automated speech recognition (ASR) stem only from acoustic or linguistic input by investigating how user behavior and adaptation may change when facing a biased system. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary ecology's 'Red Queen' hypothesis, which explains continuous adaptation in co-evolving species, the analogous solution involves viewing adaptation as a dynamic, reciprocal process. Adapted to this context, the research would investigate ASR not as a static evaluator but as one agent in a co-adaptive system. It would experimentally study how users' linguistic patterns, pronunciation, and communication strategies evolve over multiple engagements with a system known to have specific biases (e.g., against certain dialects). The core question is whether these user adaptations create a corrective feedback loop that pressures the system towards fairness, or if they lead to maladaptive cycles that entrench disparities further, providing a dynamic model of bias in human-AI interaction.

ideas.title: Identify High-Load Linguistic Features via Signal Detection and Noise Filtering
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from signal processing and communications engineering to the problem of pinpointing error-causing linguistic features. In the analogous field, engineers identify noise-corrupted components in a transmission by decomposing the signal and measuring the signal-to-noise ratio across frequency bands. Adapted to linguistics, the method would treat language production as a transmission system. The researcher would decompose spoken or written language data into discrete phonological and grammatical 'feature bands'. By statistically analyzing the disparity between target forms (clean signal) and error forms (noisy signal) for each band, the study would calculate a metric analogous to SNR to rank features by their contribution to error rates. This provides a quantitative, diagnostic framework to identify which specific linguistic features are the highest-load components, most susceptible to breakdown, thereby directly addressing the core question of which features cause observed error disparities.

ideas.title: Adapting Visual Perceptual Learning Paradigms to Probe ASR Dialect Bias
ideas.description: This idea adapts experimental paradigms from visual neuroscience and perceptual psychology, specifically perceptual adaptation studies used to probe biases in visual processing, to the domain of Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR). The analogous solution involves using controlled exposure (adaptation) to a specific stimulus class (e.g., faces from Group A) to induce a measurable bias, followed by diagnostic testing on a balanced set to quantify that bias's effect on different categories. Adapted to ASR, the research would involve designing an experiment where an ASR model's acoustic encoder is 'primed' or 'adapted' by processing a continuous sequence of speech from a particular dialect. Following this adaptation phase, the model's phoneme or word recognition accuracy would be tested on a carefully constructed diagnostic set containing phonemes from that dialect and several others. By analyzing the differential performance shifts—which phonemes or dialects show improved, degraded, or unchanged recognition—the study can systematically identify and characterize the contextual biases embedded within the ASR system's architecture. This method provides a controlled, causal way to test how prior acoustic context differentially affects the recognition of linguistic units from different dialects, moving beyond correlation to probe the representational spaces of the model itself.

ideas.title: Combine adversarial and supervised learning
ideas.description: Integrate adversarial training with maximum-likelihood pre-training for more stable learning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarial Learning for Neural Dialogue Generation

ideas.title: Scale down: Micro-task decomposition
ideas.description: Break complex NLP tasks into even simpler micro-tasks to further reduce non-expert cognitive load and cost.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cheap and Fast – But is it Good? Evaluating Non-Expert Annotations for Natural Language Tasks

ideas.title: Theoretical Phonology of Emergent ASR: Predicting Fine-Grained Capabilities from Scaling Laws
ideas.description: This research direction develops theoretical frameworks to predict not just *that* capabilities emerge in foundation ASR models with scale, but *which* micro-phonetic and phonological capabilities emerge, in what order, and why. It applies the lens of scale-driven emergence theory to the granular failure points identified by phone-level error analysis, seeking scaling laws for sub-word linguistic features. The goal is to produce a predictive map linking model scale, architecture, and training data properties to the acquisition of specific fine-grained auditory-phonetic competencies, fundamentally explaining how raw scaling leads to structured linguistic intelligence.

ideas.title: Challenge uniform corpus time slices
ideas.description: Use dynamically sized time windows based on event-driven linguistic shifts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Diachronic Word Embeddings Reveal Statistical Laws of Semantic Change

ideas.title: Adversarial Debiasing for ASR via Immunological Tolerance Mechanisms
ideas.description: This idea adapts adversarial training techniques from fair computer vision to remove demographic signals from ASR model representations by drawing an analogy from immunology. The analogous solution is the biological process of central tolerance, where the immune system learns to distinguish 'self' from 'non-self' by eliminating self-reactive cells during development. We adapt this by creating a training regimen for ASR models that mimics negative selection. During a dedicated adversarial 'tolerance' phase, the model's internal feature representations are fed to a demographic discriminator. The primary model is then adversarially trained to produce representations that 'fool' this discriminator, effectively eliminating features that strongly correlate with demographic attributes like speaker age, gender, or accent, while preserving those necessary for accurate speech recognition. This results in a model whose latent space is invariant to protected demographic characteristics.

ideas.title: Study mechanisms of discounting novel minority contributions
ideas.description: Investigate the specific social and cognitive mechanisms that cause novel work by underrepresented groups to be devalued.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Diversity–Innovation Paradox in Science

ideas.title: Disentangling Race, Class, and Dialect Using Population Genetics Admixture Mapping Techniques
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of admixture mapping from population genetics to challenge the assumption that race is a proxy for dialect alone. In genetics, admixture mapping disentangles genetic ancestry from environmental factors to attribute disease risk correctly. Adapted to sociolinguistics, we would design a large-scale study collecting speech samples and detailed demographic data. We would then use multivariate regression or structural equation modeling, treating racial identification, socio-economic indicators (e.g., income, education), and precise geographic coordinates as independent, potentially correlated, predictor variables for specific dialect features. This method statistically 'controls' for region and class to isolate the unique predictive power of racial identification on speech, thereby disentangling these conflated variables and testing the core assumption.

ideas.title: Theoretical Frameworks for Emergent Personalization in Lightweight Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research develops theoretical frameworks to explain and predict how emergent capabilities for real-time, per-speaker adaptation can arise in heavily compressed or distilled versions of large speech foundation models. It moves beyond simply scaling down a model for efficiency, instead seeking the theoretical principles—such as specific architectural inductive biases or training dynamics—that cause a small model to suddenly exhibit strong, few-shot personalization. The goal is to derive design rules for creating lightweight ASR systems that intrinsically and predictably gain the ability to rapidly adapt to individual speakers as an emergent property of their constrained structure, rather than through explicit, costly fine-tuning.

ideas.title: Scale up to global language and dialect audit

ideas.sourceTitle: Racial disparities in automated speech recognition

ideas.title: Apply co-training or multi-view learning

ideas.title: Use phylogenetic methods from biology

ideas.title: Apply meta-learning for fast adaptation

ideas.title: Optimize task design for non-expert quality

ideas.title: Challenge the one-relation-per-pair assumption
ideas.description: Allow entity pairs to express multiple relations simultaneously, moving beyond the single-relation classification framework.
ideas.sourceTitle: Distant supervision for relation extraction without labeled data

ideas.title: Benchmarking the Inclusivity Drift of Commercial ASR Systems
ideas.description: This research proposes the creation of a standardized, multi-dimensional benchmark suite built from diverse, inclusive speech corpora. This suite is then used to conduct a temporal audit of a single commercial ASR vendor's successive model releases. The core investigation is to quantify 'inclusivity drift'—measuring how error rates for different speaker demographics (e.g., accented, dialectal, or demographic groups) change across model versions, independent of overall accuracy gains. This moves beyond a static performance snapshot to analyze the equity trajectory of monolithic commercial systems, determining if updates consistently improve inclusivity or introduce regressions for underrepresented populations.

ideas.title: Analyze stereotypes beyond gender and ethnicity

ideas.title: Systematically compare HALOs across diverse tasks

ideas.sourceTitle: KTO: Model Alignment as Prospect Theoretic Optimization

ideas.title: Longitudinal Impact Cohorts for AI Governance
ideas.description: This research establishes longitudinal cohort studies to track the performance of foundation models across diverse demographic and user groups over time, specifically monitoring Automated Speech Recognition (ASR) and other key capabilities. The collected longitudinal data on performance drift, emergent biases, and economic impacts is then fed directly into an interdisciplinary (legal, ethical, economic) policy modeling framework. This enables the creation of adaptive governance mechanisms, such as dynamic compliance standards or impact-triggered auditing, that are empirically grounded in observed societal effects rather than theoretical risks. The synergy transforms passive observation into an active feedback loop, where cohort data validates policy hypotheses and policy changes are evaluated for their effectiveness in mitigating documented harms.

ideas.title: Apply ensemble methods from machine learning
ideas.description: Use ensemble learning techniques to combine multiple non-expert annotations into a robust consensus label.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cheap and Fast – But is it Good? Evaluating Non-Expert Annotations for Natural Language Tasks

ideas.title: Multi-Speaker Dialect Feature Transplantation via Voice Conversion for Ecologically Valid ASR Evaluation
ideas.description: This approach constructs a robust evaluation framework for ASR dialect bias by transplanting specific, linguistically-defined dialect features across a diverse array of speaker identities. Using a corpus of natural speech from many speakers, neural voice conversion techniques isolate and transfer features like vowel shifts or prosodic patterns from donor utterances to a variety of baseline speaker voices. This generates a large-scale test set where dialect features are decoupled from individual speaker artifacts, enabling controlled measurement of ASR performance across true community-level acoustic variation. The resulting system provides more generalizable and ecologically valid diagnostics of ASR bias than single-speaker matched-guise methods.

ideas.title: Triangulated Dialectometry: Adversarial Consensus Across ASR Systems to Isolate Genuine Variation
ideas.description: This research employs a panel of multiple, architecturally diverse ASR systems as adversarial measurers to quantify dialectal variation robustly. The core innovation is a consensus framework that contrasts the error profiles and transcription outputs of each system on the same dialect corpus, statistically isolating patterns that are consistently associated with a dialect across all systems. This method filters out the confounding noise of any single system's biases and technical failures, revealing only the dialect features that persistently challenge state-of-the-art speech recognition. The result is a more valid and generalizable map of dialect boundaries and characteristics derived from model-agnostic computational difficulty.

ideas.title: Progressive Knowledge Scaffolding: From Narrow Expertise to Robust Generalization
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid training paradigm that starts with narrow pre-training to build deep domain expertise, then systematically expands the model's capabilities. A curriculum of pre-training stages, each on a broader but semantically adjacent domain, is designed, with data selected to challenge and integrate prior knowledge. The final model is evaluated not only on its original target tasks but crucially on its performance on novel, hybrid problems that require synthesis across the learned domains, testing for robust generalization beyond simple specialization.

ideas.title: Invert the assumption of broad, general pre-training

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Analysis of ASR Lineages: Decomposing Monolithic Vendor Models into Evolutionary Branches
ideas.description: This research challenges the monolithic assumption of commercial ASR systems by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's use of phylogenetics. In biology, the variation within a species lineage is mapped via phylogenetic trees, revealing evolutionary branches and trait divergence. The analogous solution is to model a single vendor's suite of ASR models not as one entity but as an evolving lineage with branching specializations. The adapted idea applies this by constructing a performance phylogeny for a vendor like Google or Microsoft. Different model versions (v1, v2, v3), architectural families, and deployment targets are treated as evolutionary nodes. By systematically benchmarking a matrix of performance traits—including Word Error Rate across diverse dialects, latency, resource consumption, and bias metrics—across these nodes, the research will visually and quantitatively map the internal variation and evolutionary trade-offs (e.g., accuracy vs. speed, generalist vs. specialist) that are hidden when the vendor's output is viewed monolithically. This reveals the 'phylogenetic' structure within a commercial ASR ecosystem.

ideas.title: Invert the direction of knowledge flow

ideas.title: Functionally-Weighted Phonetic Error Analysis with Perceptual and Linguistic Salience
ideas.description: This approach extends micro-phonetic error analysis by introducing a multi-dimensional weighting framework that evaluates the practical significance of each phonetic confusion. The system first performs fine-grained phone-level alignment and error detection, then applies perceptual models to determine which acoustic differences are likely noticeable to human listeners. It further analyzes phonological and lexical consequences to identify which errors lead to word identity changes or semantic ambiguity. The output is a prioritized error map that highlights phonetically similar but functionally critical failure points, enabling ASR developers to focus remediation efforts on errors that actually impact usability and comprehension.

ideas.title: Benchmarks for Disparity Dynamics: Evaluating the Relationship Between Average Accuracy and Group-Wise Performance Gaps
ideas.description: This research proposes a new class of evaluation benchmarks designed not just to measure foundation model capabilities, but to rigorously test the relationship between average task performance and performance disparities across demographic groups. The benchmarks will be structured to systematically induce and measure scenarios where aggregate accuracy improves while gaps widen, remain constant, or narrow, identifying the architectural, data, and task characteristics that drive these outcomes. This enables a shift from post-hoc fairness auditing to a predictive understanding of disparity trajectories during model scaling and refinement. The resulting framework will provide actionable guidance for development strategies that explicitly couple general improvement with the reduction of performance gaps.

ideas.title: Scale up: Massively multi-agent dialogue society

ideas.title: Cohort-Adaptive Streaming: A Longitudinal Framework for Auditing and Steering Continuous Learning
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that integrates the longitudinal tracking methods of epidemiological cohort studies directly into the architecture of models learning from dynamic data streams. Instead of a static audit, defined user cohorts are embedded as persistent, instrumented entities within the learning system. Their performance metrics (e.g., ASR error rates, fairness measures) are continuously monitored as the model updates, creating a live 'epidemiological surveillance' dashboard for model health across subpopulations. This real-time cohort performance data is then used as a regulatory signal to actively bias the streaming learning algorithm—for example, by prioritizing data from cohorts experiencing performance degradation or triggering targeted retraining—thereby making the model's adaptation responsive to longitudinal equity and reliability outcomes.

ideas.title: Apply experimental audit studies

ideas.title: Incentivizing Architectural Diversity via Collective Robustness Benchmarks
ideas.description: This research designs and validates a 'Diversity Stress Test' benchmark that measures the collective robustness of a cohort of AI models with varied architectures. The benchmark's scoring mechanism explicitly rewards complementary strengths and non-correlated failure modes, creating a quantifiable performance metric for architectural diversity. The project then develops open-source tools for training and selecting models to optimize for this collective benchmark, demonstrating that diverse cohorts outperform homogeneous ones on critical safety and reliability metrics. By establishing this benchmark as a standard for high-assurance AI systems, the research aims to create a market-driven incentive for developing and deploying architecturally diverse models.
