ideas.title: Decentralized Longitudinal Observational Study of Foundation Model Performance Drift
ideas.description: This research proposes a federated, longitudinal cohort study framework to continuously monitor the performance and safety of foundation models as they are updated and fine-tuned across decentralized training ecosystems. By applying epidemiological methods within a federated learning paradigm, the study can track metrics like ASR performance, fairness, and robustness for distinct user cohorts over time without centralizing sensitive data or model weights. This enables the detection of emergent performance disparities, safety regressions, or concept drift that affect specific subpopulations, which would be missed by aggregate, centralized evaluation. The synergy yields a novel public health-inspired monitoring system for decentralized AI, providing critical auditability and accountability for the long-term societal impact of evolving models.

ideas.title: Use psychometric scaling for utility calibration
ideas.description: Employ methods from psychometrics to empirically measure and scale human utility functions for alignment.
ideas.sourceTitle: KTO: Model Alignment as Prospect Theoretic Optimization

ideas.title: Establish a 'Periodic Table' of Foundation Model Capabilities
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from psychometrics and standardized educational testing to the problem of evaluating foundation models. The analogous field solved the problem of assessing complex, multi-faceted human abilities by developing validated test batteries that decompose broad constructs into specific, measurable latent factors using rigorous statistical methodologies like factor analysis and Item Response Theory (IRT). Adapted to AI, the research would involve designing a comprehensive benchmark suite not as a collection of unrelated tasks, but as a structured 'periodic table' of capabilities. This would entail creating a large battery of diagnostic probes intended to measure hypothesized latent factors (e.g., systematic generalization, ethical reasoning, procedural knowledge). By applying IRT, the difficulty of each probe and the 'ability' of a model on each latent factor can be calibrated on a common, interpretable scale. This enables systematic, quantitative profiling of a model's capabilities and limitations, robust comparison across different architectures and training runs, and the identification of failure modes that are correlated with specific latent factor deficits, moving beyond aggregate scores to a more nuanced understanding of model composition.

ideas.title: Study long-tail prediction improvements in detail

ideas.sourceTitle: Generalization through Memorization: Nearest Neighbor Language Models

ideas.title: Adversarial Differential Testing for ASR: Iterative Bias Discovery and Removal
ideas.description: This research fuses differential testing and adversarial debiasing into an iterative framework for automatic speech recognition (ASR) fairness. First, differential testing is used to systematically mine speech inputs that cause maximal performance disparity across demographic groups, creating a potent, automatically-generated adversarial dataset. This dataset then fuels an adversarial training loop, where a debiasing network is trained to remove demographic signals from the ASR model's latent representations, specifically targeting the failure modes discovered. The hardened model is then differentially tested again, discovering new, more subtle failure cases and closing the loop for continuous, automated bias detection and mitigation.

ideas.title: Improving average accuracy reduces disparities

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust Lifelong Learning for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction develops foundation models that learn securely and robustly from non-stationary, streaming data sources. It moves beyond static dataset defense to create systems that perform real-time adversarial detection and data provenance verification on incoming data, dynamically updating the model's parameters and threat models. This continuous learning process not only adapts to new, legitimate information but also actively inoculates the model against evolving attack strategies observed in the wild. Consequently, the model's security and robustness become emergent properties of its lifelong learning mechanism, creating a more resilient and adaptable system than one trained on a fixed, pre-vetted corpus.

ideas.title: Relax turn-level reward assignment assumption

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Trial Simulation Frameworks to Evaluate ASR in High-Stakes Contexts
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from pharmaceutical clinical trials, where in silico simulation frameworks are used to model complex real-world interactions and identify risks across diverse populations before human testing. The analogous solution involves creating controlled, virtual environments to predict drug performance and safety disparities. Adapted to ASR research, the idea is to build a sophisticated simulation framework that generates synthetic audio datasets replicating the acoustic, linguistic, and situational complexities of high-stakes applications like healthcare dictation or virtual assistants. This framework would systematically vary critical factors—background noise, speaker demographics, dialects, speech styles, and emotional states—to rigorously evaluate ASR performance disparities, identify likely failure points, and benchmark system robustness in a controlled, reproducible, and ethical manner prior to real-world implementation.

ideas.title: Dual: use kNN to diagnose LM failures

ideas.title: Adversarial Robustness Evaluation of ASR in High-Stakes Application Contexts
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the intersection of adversarial robustness and real-world application performance for Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR). It moves beyond generic robustness testing by evaluating how adversarial audio perturbations—crafted to exploit model vulnerabilities—perform in the specific acoustic environments and linguistic contexts of high-stakes applications like medical dictation or command-driven virtual assistants. The goal is to develop application-aware adversarial evaluation frameworks that reveal domain-specific attack surfaces and failure modes, thereby informing the development of more targeted and practical defense mechanisms. This synergy bridges the gap between abstract security research and applied performance testing, ultimately aiming to harden foundation ASR models against exploits that could have serious consequences in deployed systems.

ideas.title: Disentangling Acoustic Mismatch from Contextual Bias in ASR Using Synthesized Dialect Stimuli and Model Probing
ideas.description: This research employs high-fidelity speech synthesis to generate phonetically controlled stimulus sets, creating acoustically balanced primes and targets across multiple dialects. It then runs priming experiments within a state-of-the-art ASR system, using both traditional output analysis and direct probing of the model's internal hidden representations. By comparing the effects of context on these internal layers versus final transcriptions, the method can isolate whether dialect-based recognition errors originate from early acoustic-phonetic mismatches or from later, context-influenced lexical integration stages. This approach provides a causal, mechanistic account of bias in ASR, moving beyond correlational observations.

ideas.title: Assume ambiguous or overlapping semantic roles

ideas.title: Longitudinal Fairness Auditing: Tracking Demographic Disparity Evolution Across Vendor Model Lineages
ideas.description: This research fuses the analysis of intra-vendor model evolution with standardized fairness auditing to create a longitudinal study framework. It proposes to apply a consistent, multi-dimensional fairness benchmark suite not just across different vendors, but sequentially across the historical and current model versions released by a single vendor (e.g., Whisper v1 to v3, Google USM iterations). This enables tracking how demographic disparity metrics for specific accent, gender, and age groups change—intentionally or unintentionally—with each model update. The core novelty is in using the standardized benchmark as a controlled measurement tool to isolate and attribute fairness fluctuations to specific vendor decisions like training data curation shifts, architectural changes, or fine-tuning objectives, thereby holding vendors accountable for the fairness trajectory of their product line.

ideas.title: Adapting Psychophysics' Method of Constant Stimuli for Dialect Feature Synthesis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Method of Constant Stimuli from psychophysics to the sociolinguistic matched-guise technique for ASR testing. In psychophysics, this method uses a fixed set of stimuli varying systematically in one physical property (e.g., sound frequency) to measure perceptual thresholds. The analogous solution is adapted by synthesizing a controlled continuum of speech guises where only one sociolinguistic dialect feature (such as vowel centralization or stop-glottalization) is parametrically manipulated across a defined range of values. This creates a stimulus set of synthetic voices that are identical except for this one graduated feature. These samples are then presented in a randomized sequence to an ASR engine, allowing researchers to precisely quantify how incremental changes in a specific dialectal variable affect word error rates, thereby identifying critical thresholds or nonlinearities in ASR performance with a level of control akin to sensory experimentation.

ideas.title: All non-experts are equally non-expert

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Error Analysis: Using ASR Bias as a Signal for Dialect Topology
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from evolutionary biology—using systematic mutation patterns to reconstruct phylogenies—to the study of dialect. In the original idea, ASR error patterns are a lens for dialectal variation. Here, we explicitly treat a biased ASR system as an artificial, selective 'listener' whose consistent transcription errors for different dialects serve as analogous to genetic mutations. By applying phylogenetic tree-building algorithms and genetic distance metrics (e.g., based on Levenshtein distance applied to error types) to matrices of these ASR 'mutations,' we can create a novel, data-driven topology of dialect relationships. This method quantifies dialectal variation not just in terms of error rates, but as a structured evolutionary-like landscape, revealing how dialects cluster and diverge based on the systematic biases of a technological listener, offering a complementary perspective to traditional linguistic atlas surveys.

ideas.title: Personalized ASR via On-Device Immune-Style Memory
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from computational immunology, where the adaptive immune system uses a memory of past pathogens to mount efficient, targeted responses. The analogous problem in ASR is the high cost of per-speaker fine-tuning. The adapted idea is to develop a lightweight, real-time ASR personalization system inspired by immune memory. A small, on-device 'memory network' will store and recall compact, speaker-specific acoustic features. Upon recognizing a known speaker, this module activates to provide a contextual bias to a frozen base ASR model, significantly reducing error rates for that individual with minimal latency and power consumption, effectively creating a scalable, personalized secondary response without full model retraining.

ideas.title: Neural Plasticity-Inspired Models for Continuous Learning from Data Streams
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that massive, static datasets are necessary for training capable models by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience. In biological brains, lifelong learning from a continuous stream of experiences is solved through mechanisms of synaptic plasticity, metaplasticity, and complementary learning systems—not through repeated exposure to fixed, massive datasets. The adapted idea is to develop machine learning architectures that implement analogous computational rules: sparse, event-driven parameter updates (akin to Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity) triggered by data novelty and prediction error in a streaming context, combined with metaplastic mechanisms that dynamically protect consolidated knowledge by adjusting per-parameter learning rates based on update history. This approach would enable models to learn continuously from dynamic data sources (e.g., sensor streams, real-time transactions, evolving social media) while maintaining stability and avoiding catastrophic forgetting, fundamentally moving away from the paradigm of static dataset curation and batch retraining.

ideas.title: Federated Mixture of Local Experts (FMLE): A Composable Architecture for Decentralized Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research designs a federated system where participants train and maintain specialized expert models on their local data, never sharing raw data or full model weights. A separate, lightweight routing model is trained via secure federated learning across the network to learn which combination of experts to invoke for a given task or query. Inference is performed by the routing model calling upon the relevant subset of experts, with their outputs composed to form a complete response. This approach eliminates the core communication bottleneck of synchronized training while creating a scalable, composable, and privacy-preserving alternative to a monolithic foundation model.

ideas.title: A Statistical Mechanics Framework for Predicting Phase Transitions in Model Capabilities
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to predicting phase transitions in condensed matter systems to the problem of emergent capabilities in AI. In statistical physics, frameworks like renormalization group theory use order parameters and scaling laws to explain how macroscopic properties (like magnetism) emerge from microscopic interactions and to predict critical points of change. The adapted idea is to create a theoretical framework for foundation models that defines analogous 'capability order parameters' (quantitative metrics for skills like in-context learning or chain-of-thought reasoning) and treats model scale (parameters, data, compute) as the control parameter. The goal is to develop scaling laws that predict critical thresholds where new capabilities undergo sharp transitions, and to classify these emergent phenomena into universality classes based on model architecture and training data properties, providing a principled theory for capability prediction.

ideas.title: Mitigate AI Homogenization via Portfolio Theory and Architectural Hedging
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to systemic risk from Modern Portfolio Theory in finance to the problem of architectural homogenization in AI. In finance, a diversified portfolio of uncorrelated assets hedges against market-wide crashes. The analogous solution for AI is to intentionally cultivate and operationalize a diverse portfolio of model architectures, rather than converging on a single dominant design. The adapted research idea involves developing formal methods to quantify the 'correlation' of failure modes and performance profiles across different AI architectures (e.g., transformers, state-space models, diffusion models, neurosymbolic systems). Using these metrics, it would create frameworks for constructing and managing ensembles or federations of models, dynamically allocating tasks to this portfolio to maximize robustness and minimize systemic risk from any single architectural flaw or adversarial attack vector, thereby 'hedging' the AI ecosystem's bets.

ideas.title: Immunological Training for Foundation Models: Anomaly Detection and Response Inspired by Adaptive Immunity
ideas.description: This research adapts principles from immunology, specifically the vertebrate adaptive immune system, to improve the security and robustness of foundation models against adversarial attacks and data poisoning. The analogous solution involves the immune system's layered defense: innate, non-specific barriers coupled with an adaptive system that learns, remembers, and even has a selection process (negative selection) to avoid self-targeting. The adapted idea is to design a security architecture for foundation models that mimics this structure. This involves creating an 'innate immunity' module of static, broad-spectrum anomaly and perturbation detectors. Concurrently, an 'adaptive immunity' subsystem would be trained through continuous exposure to attack simulations, learning to recognize new adversarial patterns. Crucially, inspired by negative selection in the thymus, the model would undergo a security-focused fine-tuning phase designed to identify and suppress internal parameters or feature representations that are disproportionately activated by poisoned data or lead to harmful, unintended outputs, thereby building a more resilient and self-defending model.

ideas.title: Neuro-Symbolic Continual Learning from Data Streams
ideas.description: This research direction develops hybrid architectures where a neural component learns statistical patterns from a dynamic data stream, while a tightly coupled symbolic module extracts, refines, and maintains explicit knowledge structures (e.g., rules, ontologies, causal graphs) from these patterns in real-time. The symbolic component provides a stable, interpretable memory that guides the neural network's adaptation to new data, preventing catastrophic forgetting and enabling deliberate reasoning over accumulated knowledge. This closed-loop system allows for continuous model refinement and hypothesis testing, moving beyond pattern recognition to build evolving, causal world models directly from experiential, non-i.i.d. data streams.

ideas.title: Scale as a Discovery Engine for Fundamental Efficiency
ideas.description: This research program strategically employs large-scale models as automated, simulation-based research instruments to discover the fundamental algorithmic and architectural innovations required for advanced capabilities. Instead of directly pursuing efficient end-models, the system uses scale to exhaustively explore a vast space of training paradigms, data structures, and model designs in silico, identifying the minimal sufficient conditions for target capabilities. The final output is a set of proven, efficient blueprints and generative principles that are decoupled from the initial scale used to find them, ensuring the path to capability is both efficient and robust to unknown scaling laws.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Foundation Models via Real-World Application Proxies
ideas.description: This research proposes a new paradigm for benchmarking foundation models by constructing detailed, interactive simulation environments based on high-stakes real-world applications, such as clinical documentation or legal proceedings. Instead of evaluating isolated capabilities on static datasets, these 'application proxy benchmarks' test the model's integrated performance in complex, multi-step tasks that require understanding context, managing uncertainty, and adhering to domain-specific constraints. This approach systematically reveals failure modes related to robustness, bias, and safety that only emerge under realistic task pressures and interaction patterns, providing a more predictive assessment of real-world utility and risk than current abstract benchmarks.

ideas.title: Prospective Randomized Challenge Trials for Pre-Deployment ASR Model Evaluation
ideas.description: This research system combines longitudinal cohort tracking with prospective, interventional trials. Prior to a full model update rollout, a randomized subset of existing, demographically-stratified user cohorts is exposed to the new model version in a controlled, logged environment that mirrors real use. This creates a pre-post, treatment-control experimental design, enabling causal inference about the update's impact on performance and fairness across groups. The method shifts evaluation from passive, post-hoc observation to active, pre-deployment risk assessment, allowing developers to mitigate issues before they affect the entire user population.

ideas.title: Decentralized Foundation Model Training via Distributed Proof-of-Work Consensus
ideas.description: This research seed inverts the centralization of model development by exploring a fully decentralized training paradigm for foundation models, inspired by blockchain consensus mechanisms. The analogous field is Cryptocurrency and Distributed Ledger Technology, where networks like Bitcoin achieve secure, trustless consensus without a central authority through incentive-aligned Proof-of-Work. The adapted idea proposes a system where a global network of compute nodes trains a foundation model collaboratively. Each node trains on a local, private data shard and participates in a consensus mechanism to propose updates to the global model parameters. These updates are verified by the network through a delegated validation task, and nodes that contribute useful updates are rewarded with a cryptographic token. This creates a sustainable, incentive-driven ecosystem for decentralized training that challenges the core assumption that foundation model development must be controlled by centralized entities with massive capital and data aggregation.

ideas.title: Architectures for Stable, Continual Learning with Protected Core Knowledge
ideas.description: This research designs neural architectures that enable continuous learning from dynamic streams while explicitly preventing catastrophic forgetting and corruption of core knowledge. The system features a dual-memory structure, separating a stable, foundational knowledge base from a flexible working memory for new data. A learned consolidation gate, driven by predictive uncertainty and alignment with protected principles, controls irreversible updates to the core model. This allows the model to adapt to non-stationary data distributions online while guaranteeing the integrity of its original, vetted capabilities and safety alignment.

ideas.title: Hybrid Neuro-Symbolic Architecture with a 'Consensus Interface'
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of end-to-end differentiability by investigating hybrid architectures. Drawing an analogy from computational biology's protein folding solutions, we adapt the concept of a 'consensus interface.' In protein prediction, stochastic search and knowledge-based filters cooperate via a shared 3D representation and consensus scoring, not backpropagation. The adapted idea proposes a neuro-symbolic architecture where a neural network (for perception/pattern completion) and a symbolic module (for logic/constraint satisfaction) operate independently. They exchange proposals through a shared, interpretable data structure (like a scene graph or program sketch). A separate consensus module, using a joint utility function, evaluates, selects, or merges these proposals to iteratively refine the solution, creating a cooperative, non-differentiable pipeline.

ideas.description: This research designs and validates a mandatory adversarial testing regime for high-stakes foundation models, moving beyond theoretical frameworks. It develops standardized, scenario-based 'Societal Stress Tests' that probe legal compliance, ethical boundaries, and economic externalities through simulated deployment environments. The system creates independent auditing institutions with legal authority to conduct these tests, producing binding reports that directly influence regulatory licensing, liability shields, and deployment permits. This transforms governance from abstract principles into a concrete, enforceable pre-deployment checkpoint.

ideas.title: Efficient Model Design via Evolutionary Pressure and Environmental Constraint
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from evolutionary biology, where organisms in constrained environments evolve extreme efficiency rather than size. The analogous problem—achieving fitness without massive resources—is solved through evolutionary pressure and specialization to a niche. We adapt this by pursuing research into highly efficient models that match capabilities without massive scale. Instead of scaling model size and data volume, we propose imposing artificial 'environmental constraints' during training, such as severe computational budgets, limited data access, or specific, hard failure modes. The research will systematically study how different constraint regimes (akin to different evolutionary niches) force the emergence of highly efficient, specialized, and robust architectures or learning algorithms, aiming to discover design principles for capability that are orthogonal to scale.

ideas.title: A Precautionary, Adaptive Governance Framework for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'precautionary principle' and 'adaptive management' frameworks from environmental science—used to govern technologies like GMOs—to the problem of foundation model deployment. The analogous solution integrates continuous monitoring, staged approvals, and trigger-based interventions to manage uncertain systemic risks. The adapted idea creates an interdisciplinary governance mechanism that legally mandates staged deployment licenses, economically incentivizes compliance through market access, and is ethically guided by continuous impact assessments. A core output is a dynamic policy framework that evolves based on observed societal impacts from a dedicated monitoring observatory, ensuring legal, ethical, and economic research is operationally integrated into the model lifecycle.
